law.theory_description: Agents that play text games using language models are best served by a hierarchical memory architecture that separates (1) detailed episodic traces (raw transcripts, recent observations, exact action sequences), (2) compressed schemas/semantic summaries (location types, object affordances, quest templates), and (3) prospective (intention/subgoal) memory holding short conditional plans. The hierarchy trades off recoverable detail for generalization and supports efficient retrieval, long-horizon credit assignment, and reuse of strategies under partial observability and limited retrieval bandwidth.

law.theory_statement: Explicit storage of short prospective plans (conditional subgoal sequences of length L ≈ 2–8, with attached verification hooks) reduces expected re-planning cost CR roughly proportional to the fraction φ of repeated subgoals successfully retained and reused: CR ∝ (1 - φ). Storing prospective plans improves average-step efficiency and reduces repeated exploratory backtracking when plans are reliably verified upon retrieval.
law.domain_scope: Applies when agents encounter recurring multi-step subroutines (e.g., fetch-assemble-use workflows, door-unlock sequences) in text games, and when plan execution can be interrupted or requires recovery after exploration; assumes plans are stored with contextual triggers (state fingerprints) enabling targeted retrieval.
special_cases: If plan outcomes are highly non-deterministic or environment dynamics change between attempts, retained plans may mislead and increase wasted actions.
Adversarial paraphrasing or deliberate state obfuscation without robust state fingerprints reduces utility of stored plans.
Very long prospective plans (L≫8) become brittle and costly to maintain; effectiveness concentrates on short chunks.

supporting_evidence.text: Hierarchical RL/options literature shows reusable subpolicies reduce planning overhead; episodic control caches successful trajectories for faster reuse.
Human prospective memory research demonstrates that storing intentions reduces planning load and improves task completion rates in interrupted tasks.
Practical text-game agents that cache short successful action sequences or recipes empirically show efficiency gains on recurring quests.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Options and subpolicy reuse are standard in RL; simple action-sequence caching has been used in text-game agents.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Formalizing prospective memory as a first-class memory partition for LM agents with a compact predictive relationship for re-planning cost (CR ∝ (1 - φ)) and prescribing verification hooks and conditional triggers tied to language fingerprints.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This extends existing hierarchical/subgoal reuse ideas into a memory engineering principle for LM-controller agents interacting through text.
references: Sutton et al. (1999) Between MDPs and Semi-MDPs: Options [subgoal reuse]
Blundell et al. (2016) Model-Free Episodic Control [trajectory caching]
Bower & Morrow (1990) Prospective memory studies [human evidence for intention storage]

evaluation.explanation: The core claim that storing reusable subgoal sequences reduces re-planning cost aligns well with established findings in hierarchical RL (options framework), episodic control, and human prospective memory research. The proportional relationship CR ∝ (1 - φ) is a reasonable first-order approximation, though real systems show more complex dependencies on context-matching quality and environmental stochasticity. The theory correctly identifies the key mechanisms and their dependencies, with appropriate caveats about non-deterministic environments.

The law provides concrete parameters (plan length L ≈ 2–8, retention fraction φ, re-planning cost CR) and specifies architectural requirements (verification hooks, state fingerprints, contextual triggers). The proportionality relationship CR ∝ (1 - φ) is mathematically explicit though simplified. Domain scope is clearly articulated with specific examples (fetch-assemble-use, door-unlock sequences). Some implementation details (exact fingerprinting methods, verification hook mechanisms) remain underspecified but the overall framework is operationalizable.

The core mechanism of reusable subgoal sequences is well-established in hierarchical RL (options/skills) and episodic control literature. The novelty lies in the explicit synthesis and formalization for LM-based text-game agents, particularly the integration of verification hooks with language-based state fingerprints and the explicit cost model CR ∝ (1 - φ). This represents a moderately novel application and unification of known principles to a specific architectural context rather than a fundamentally new mechanism.

The theory makes clear, falsifiable predictions: agents with prospective memory should show reduced re-planning episodes, faster task completion on recurring subroutines, and efficiency gains proportional to plan reuse rate φ. Concrete experimental interventions are straightforward: compare agents with/without prospective memory on text games with recurring multi-step tasks, measure re-planning frequency, action efficiency, and success rates. The specific parameter ranges (L ≈ 2–8) and cost relationship provide quantitative testable predictions.

The mechanism is highly plausible and well-grounded in multiple established frameworks: hierarchical RL demonstrates subpolicy reuse reduces planning cost, episodic control shows trajectory caching improves efficiency, and human prospective memory research confirms intention storage aids task completion. The proposed proportional cost relationship is a reasonable approximation consistent with option-reuse models. The specified failure modes (non-deterministic environments, state obfuscation) align with known limitations of plan-reuse approaches.

While the underlying mechanisms (options, episodic control, prospective memory) have substantial empirical support in their respective domains, the specific formulation for LM-based text-game agents with the proposed architecture (verification hooks, state fingerprints, CR ∝ (1 - φ) relationship) appears not to have been directly tested. The theory cites practical text-game agents showing efficiency gains from action-sequence caching, but this is indirect evidence rather than systematic evaluation of the full proposed framework with its specific components and quantitative predictions.

law.theory_description: A unified, differentiable control policy for episodic key-value memory in language-model (LM) agents playing text games. UDDMC gates writes based on probabilistic surprise multiplied by an estimator of expected future utility, modulated by an explicit storage cost; it controls retrieval concentration via a learned mapping from epistemic uncertainty to softmax temperature; and it implements end-to-end differentiable reads/writes so reward gradients backpropagate to memory-addressing and content. The result is sparse, high-utility memory contents, focused retrieval when the agent is uncertain, and improved long-horizon credit assignment for textual decision sequences.

law.theory_statement: Memory retrieval attention scores use softmax(key·query / tau(u)), where tau(u) is a learned, differentiable temperature function of epistemic uncertainty u about the current state; tau(u) monotonically decreases with u, producing sharper (more concentrated) retrieval distributions when the agent is more uncertain, and broader retrieval (higher tau) when the agent is confident to expose richer context.
law.domain_scope: Applies where (1) memory retrieval is implemented by differentiable attention over key-value stores, (2) per-step token budget limits the number of retrieved tokens that can condition the LM, and (3) an epistemic uncertainty estimate is available (e.g., ensemble variance, MC-dropout variance, logits-entropy calibrated by augmentations). Not applicable when no credible uncertainty estimate can be computed or when retrieval must be hard/one-hot by design.
special_cases: Poor or adversarial uncertainty estimates may cause overconfident or underconfident retrieval; calibration procedures or robust estimators (ensembles, deep ensembles) are recommended.
Very low tau (near-zero) can cause brittle single-memory reliance; add entropy regularization or floor temperature to avoid collapse.
When retrieval budget is large (effectively unlimited), temperature control is less impactful and can be neutralized.

supporting_evidence.text: Softmax temperature controls distribution concentration; used widely in attention and sampling.

Uncertainty-aware attention and selection have precedent in active learning and some memory/attention works; LM uncertainty signals (ensembles, MC-dropout, deep ensembles) are practical and informative for calibration.
Retrieval-augmented LMs (RAG, RETRO) show the importance of selecting high-quality contexts — concentration on few high-value contexts is beneficial when retrieval is costly.
Calibration and ensemble techniques (Lakshminarayanan et al. 2017; Gal & Ghahramani 2016) provide operational uncertainty metrics suitable for tau(u).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Temperature scaling and uncertainty estimation are established; attention mechanisms and retrieval-augmented LMs use scoring functions.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A continuous differentiable mapping from LM epistemic uncertainty to retrieval temperature tau, used as an operational knob to trade off concentration vs. breadth under token budgets for text-game agents.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The components exist separately; coupling uncertainty to retrieval temperature as a learned differentiable module targeted to token-limited gameplay is novel and actionable.
references: Gal & Ghahramani (2016) Dropout as a Bayesian Approximation [MC-dropout uncertainty]
Lakshminarayanan et al. (2017) Deep Ensembles [practical uncertainty]
Lewis et al. (2020) RAG [retrieval selection]
Borgeaud et al. (2022) RETRO [retrieval-augmented LM]

evaluation.explanation: The core claims are factually sound: softmax temperature does control distribution concentration, uncertainty estimates from ensembles/MC-dropout are established and validated approaches, and retrieval-augmented LMs do benefit from selective context retrieval. The inverse relationship (lower tau for higher uncertainty to concentrate retrieval) is mechanistically coherent given token budget constraints. The supporting references (Gal & Ghahramani 2016, Lakshminarayanan et al. 2017, Lewis et al. 2020 RAG, Borgeaud et al. 2022 RETRO) are real and accurately characterized.

The mechanism is well-specified: softmax(key·query / tau(u)) with explicit functional form, clear inputs (epistemic uncertainty u), and concrete operationalization options (ensemble variance, MC-dropout, entropy calibration). The monotonic decreasing relationship between tau and u is stated explicitly. However, the exact functional form of tau(u) is not specified (linear, exponential, learned neural network?), and calibration procedures are mentioned but not detailed, leaving some implementation ambiguity.

The individual components—softmax temperature control, uncertainty estimation, attention mechanisms, retrieval-augmented LMs—are all established. The novelty lies in the specific integration: using epistemic uncertainty as a continuous, differentiable control signal for retrieval temperature in token-budget-constrained text-game agents. This is a moderately novel synthesis that addresses a practical problem (balancing retrieval breadth vs. concentration under constraints) in a way not previously unified in the literature, but builds clearly on existing primitives.

The theory makes clear, falsifiable predictions: (1) retrieval distributions should be more concentrated (lower entropy) when uncertainty is high, (2) performance on long-horizon text games should improve compared to fixed-temperature baselines, (3) ablations varying tau(u) vs. constant tau should show differential performance. Experiments can straightforwardly measure uncertainty (ensemble variance), retrieval entropy, and task performance. The domain (text-game agents with key-value memory) is concrete and accessible for empirical testing.

The mechanism is plausible and aligns with known principles: high uncertainty signals ambiguity, which justifies focusing on the most relevant single context (lower tau) to avoid diluting limited token budgets with many weak signals. Conversely, low uncertainty allows broader exploration (higher tau). This is consistent with active learning intuitions and efficient information use. However, the inverse relationship (sharper retrieval when uncertain) is somewhat counterintuitive compared to exploration strategies that broaden search under uncertainty; the rationale (token budget constraints favoring precision over breadth) is valid but context-dependent.

The theory itself appears to be a proposed mechanism rather than one with extensive published empirical validation. While the components (uncertainty estimation, temperature-scaled attention) have been tested separately in other contexts, the specific integration—learned tau(u) mapping from epistemic uncertainty to retrieval temperature in text-game agents with token budgets—has minimal direct empirical coverage in the literature. The theory is testable but not yet extensively tested in its stated domain.

UDDMC gates writes based on probabilistic surprise multiplied by an estimator of expected future utility, modulated by an explicit storage cost; it controls retrieval concentration via a learned mapping from epistemic uncertainty to softmax temperature; and it implements end-to-end differentiable reads/writes so reward gradients backpropagate to memory-addressing and content. The result is sparse, high-utility memory contents, focused retrieval when the agent is uncertain, and improved long-horizon credit assignment for textual decision sequences.

law.statement_name: Differentiable Credit Assignment Through Memory Replay
law.theory_statement: Making write and read operations soft/differentiable allows gradients from delayed textual rewards to flow back through retrieval attention weights and write gates to the original stored encodings; this lowers variance of long-horizon credit signals and enables the model to learn which observations causally influenced future returns, improving sample-efficiency over purely heuristic, non-differentiable replay scheduling.
law.domain_scope: Applies to agents trained with gradient-based learning (supervised fine-tuning, actor-critic/policy gradients with reparameterization where possible) and using soft attention addressing in memory modules (e.g., Neural Turing Machine, DNC, Memory Networks). Not directly applicable to hard-coded episodic buffers with discrete addresses unless relaxed differentiably.
special_cases: On-policy RL with non-differentiable sampling (e.g., discrete action sampling without reparameterization) still requires variance reduction techniques (baselines/advantage).
Very long horizons can make end-to-end differentiation computationally heavy; truncated backpropagation through time or episodic credit proxies may be necessary.
Where reward sparsity is extreme, gradients through memory alone may be insufficient without additional auxiliary losses (behavior cloning, value prediction).

supporting_evidence.text: Differentiable memory architectures (NTM, DNC, End-to-end Memory Networks) allow gradient flow through read/write addressing and improve tasks requiring long-term dependencies.

Non-differentiable prioritized replay requires heuristics to assign importance and cannot directly receive gradient signals from future returns; prioritized replay still effective but limited for direct credit assignment.

Empirical RL literature shows that structured memory and attention mechanisms can reduce variance and improve learning on long-horizon tasks when integrated with gradients (Graves et al. 2016; Kaiser et al. works).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Differentiable memory architectures and prioritized replay are established; applying gradient-based credit assignment through attention/read-write mechanisms is known in sequence modeling.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Framing differentiable read/write gating specifically as the mechanism for long-horizon credit-assignment in LM agents for text games, combined with the uncertainty-driven write/retrieval controls of UDDMC.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Integrates known differentiable-memory advantages into a reward-driven LM context and prescribes concrete mechanisms for credit flow tied to surprise and utility gating.
references: Graves et al. (2016) Differentiable Neural Computer [differentiable memory]
Kaiser et al. (2017) Learning to remember rare events [memory and rare events]
Schaul et al. (2015) Prioritized Experience Replay [non-differentiable prioritization]

evaluation.explanation: The core claim that differentiable memory operations enable gradient flow for credit assignment is factually correct and well-established in neural architecture literature (NTM, DNC, Memory Networks). The assertion that this reduces variance compared to non-differentiable discrete addressing aligns with known results showing that soft attention mechanisms provide smoother optimization landscapes. The claim about prioritized replay being limited to heuristic importance assignment is accurate. Minor deduction because the comparative advantage claim ('improving sample-efficiency over purely heuristic... replay') while plausible, is context-dependent and not universally demonstrated across all RL settings.

The theory specifies the mechanism (gradient backpropagation through soft read/write operations) and identifies key architectural requirements (soft attention addressing, reparameterization). It clearly delineates applicable training regimes (gradient-based learning) and architectural constraints (differentiable addressing). However, it lacks quantitative predictions about the magnitude of variance reduction or sample-efficiency gains, and doesn't specify concrete metrics or thresholds that would make experimental validation more precise. The special cases provide useful operational boundaries.

The theory correctly identifies itself as 'somewhat-related-to-existing' work. Differentiable memory architectures enabling gradient flow are well-established (Graves et al. 2016, Sukhbaatar et al. 2015). The application to credit assignment in RL with memory is explored in prior work (Kaiser et al. 2017). The novelty lies primarily in the specific framing for LM agents in text games and integration with the broader UDDMC framework's uncertainty-driven gating mechanisms. This represents a reasonable synthesis and domain-specific application rather than a fundamentally new principle.

The theory makes clear, testable predictions: agents with differentiable memory should show lower gradient variance and better sample efficiency on long-horizon tasks compared to non-differentiable discrete memory or heuristic replay. Concrete experimental designs are feasible—compare differentiable vs. non-differentiable memory variants on text games while measuring gradient variance, sample efficiency, and long-horizon task performance. The theory identifies specific architectural requirements (soft attention) that can be ablated. Deduction because quantitative thresholds for 'improved' performance aren't specified, leaving some ambiguity in falsification criteria.

The mechanism is highly plausible and grounded in established principles of automatic differentiation and neural network optimization. Differentiable operations enabling gradient flow is a fundamental property of modern deep learning. The claim that smoother, continuous addressing reduces variance compared to discrete operations aligns with optimization theory and empirical observations in attention mechanisms. The acknowledgment of limitations (computational cost at long horizons, need for auxiliary losses) demonstrates realistic understanding. Well-aligned with known mechanisms in both memory-augmented networks and RL credit assignment.

While the underlying components (differentiable memory architectures, soft attention) have been empirically validated in their original contexts, the specific claim about credit assignment advantages in LM agents for text games appears less extensively tested. The referenced works (Graves 2016, Kaiser 2017) demonstrate differentiable memory benefits but not necessarily this specific formulation combining uncertainty-driven gating with gradient-based credit assignment in the stated domain. The comparative claim against 'purely heuristic replay scheduling' in text-game contexts lacks extensive empirical coverage in the literature. More direct empirical validation of the integrated UDDMC framework would be needed.

law.theory_description: Agents that play text games using language models are best served by a hierarchical memory architecture that separates (1) detailed episodic traces (raw transcripts, recent observations, exact action sequences), (2) compressed schemas/semantic summaries (location types, object affordances, quest templates), and (3) prospective (intention/subgoal) memory holding short conditional plans. The hierarchy trades off recoverable detail for generalization and supports efficient retrieval, long-horizon credit assignment, and reuse of strategies under partial observability and limited retrieval bandwidth.

law.theory_statement: When per-step retrieval tokens/time are constrained and retrieval precision does not improve with storage, marginal performance gain ΔP from increasing raw episodic memory |E| follows diminishing returns approximated by ΔP/Δ|E| ∝ 1/|E| (i.e., P ∝ log |E|) beyond an initial regime; allocating added capacity to compressed schemas S yields larger early returns when environment structure repeats.
law.domain_scope: Applies to transformer-based LM agents with (a) hard per-decision context token limits and (b) retrieval systems whose precision (hit-rate for relevant entries) is approximately constant as |E| grows; environments must contain repeated structure so that compressed schemas capture recurring information.
special_cases: If indexing/retrieval precision improves with capacity (learned keys, hierarchical indices), returns can exceed logarithmic behavior.
If episodic entries are highly redundant, effective |E| is smaller and the law overestimates marginal benefits.
If retrieval budget is effectively unlimited, diminishing-returns constraint is relaxed.

supporting_evidence.text: Empirical RAG and nearest-neighbor cache studies show large early gains from small, well-chosen retrieval sets with tapering improvements as raw cache grows without better indexing.
General scaling/diminishing-return patterns for models and datasets (Kaplan et al.) and intuition from information theory about marginal information in random additional transcripts.
Practical constraints in deployed LMs: token windows, latency budgets, and cost-per-token which limit effective retrieval per step.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Scaling laws and caching performance curves are well-documented; retrieval literature notes tapering gains.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Applying a specific logarithmic-style diminishing-returns guideline to episodic memory for LM agents in bounded-retrieval text-game settings and deriving an actionable recommendation to prioritize compressed schema capacity.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is an application/extension of existing scaling concepts into memory-augmentation engineering for LM-driven interactive agents.
references: Kaplan et al. (2020) Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models [diminishing returns in model/data scaling]
Khandelwal et al. (2020) Nearest Neighbor Language Models [cache/retrieval performance patterns]
Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) [benefit from small retrieved contexts]

evaluation.explanation: The core claim that retrieval performance exhibits diminishing returns with increasing raw storage under fixed retrieval budgets aligns with well-established findings in information retrieval and RAG systems. The logarithmic scaling approximation is a reasonable first-order model consistent with information-theoretic principles about marginal information content. However, the specific ΔP/Δ|E| ∝ 1/|E| form is somewhat simplified and may not capture the full complexity of real retrieval systems, where precision-recall tradeoffs and redundancy can produce different curves. The claim about compressed schemas yielding better early returns when structure repeats is plausible and consistent with schema theory in cognitive science and compression principles.

The law provides a concrete mathematical form (ΔP/Δ|E| ∝ 1/|E|) and clearly identifies key variables (episodic memory size |E|, schemas S, performance gain ΔP, retrieval token limits). The domain scope is well-specified with explicit constraints (transformer-based LMs, hard context limits, constant retrieval precision, repeated environment structure). However, critical operational details remain underspecified: how to measure 'performance gain' P, what constitutes 'compressed schemas,' how to quantify 'retrieval precision,' and what threshold defines the 'initial regime' before diminishing returns begin. These ambiguities limit immediate experimental operationalization.

The theory explicitly acknowledges its foundations in well-known scaling laws (Kaplan et al.), RAG literature (Lewis et al.), and nearest-neighbor retrieval studies (Khandelwal et al.). The novelty lies in synthesizing these existing principles into a specific prescription for memory architecture design in LM-based agents playing text games. The logarithmic scaling law for episodic memory is a relatively straightforward application of known diminishing-returns patterns. The recommendation to prioritize compressed schemas represents a moderate synthesis rather than a fundamentally new mechanism, positioning this as an engineering-focused application of established principles to a specific (but not entirely novel) domain.

The law makes concrete, falsifiable predictions: varying episodic memory size |E| under fixed retrieval budgets should produce performance curves with diminishing marginal returns approximating logarithmic growth, while early allocation to schema capacity should outperform pure episodic expansion in structured environments. These predictions are testable through controlled experiments with LM agents in text games, varying memory allocations and measuring task performance. The domain scope provides clear boundary conditions (transformer-based agents, token limits, constant retrieval precision). However, some measurement ambiguities (defining 'compressed schemas,' quantifying retrieval precision, identifying regime boundaries) slightly reduce the crispness of falsification paths.

The proposed mechanism is strongly plausible and well-aligned with multiple established principles. Diminishing returns from expanding raw storage under fixed retrieval bandwidth follows directly from information theory (marginal information decreases with redundancy) and empirical RAG/retrieval studies. The logarithmic approximation is a standard model for such curves. The advantage of compressed schemas when environments contain repeated structure aligns with well-understood compression principles, cognitive schema theory, and transfer learning. The constraints (context windows, retrieval precision limits) reflect real deployment characteristics of transformer-based LMs. The mechanism is consistent with known behavior across information retrieval, machine learning scaling, and cognitive memory systems.

While the theory cites general empirical patterns from RAG, scaling laws, and nearest-neighbor retrieval studies, these provide only indirect support rather than direct tests of the specific law in its stated domain (LM agents in text games with hierarchical episodic-prospective memory). The referenced work (Kaplan, Khandelwal, Lewis) demonstrates related diminishing-returns phenomena but does not specifically test the ΔP/Δ|E| ∝ 1/|E| relationship for episodic memory vs. compressed schemas in text-game agents under the stated constraints. No direct empirical evaluation of this particular scaling law in the target domain is evident, though the supporting evidence suggests the underlying patterns are observable in related settings.

law.theory_name: Hierarchical Episodic-Semantic Memory (HESM) for Language-Model Agents
law.theory_description: A structured memory architecture that separates short-term working memory (recent tokens and actions), an episodic store of sparse, time-stamped experiences, and a semantic graph (grounded facts and abstractions). HESM prescribes consolidation rules (when and how episodic traces are abstracted into semantic graph nodes), retrieval arbitration across levels based on task-context and utility, and grounded alignment between textual episodes and semantic nodes so that LMs can generalize past episodic outcomes to novel but structurally similar quests.

law.theory_statement: An episodic trace e is consolidated into a semantic node if (A) its observed utility across episodes exceeds U_thresh (average episodic return contribution), and (B) its structural form recurs across k >= k_min distinct episodes or can be abstracted via schema induction; consolidation is accompanied by a linking mechanism storing provenance pointers to representative episodic traces.
law.domain_scope: Applies during offline or low-activity phases (analogous to sleep/replay) or during explicit consolidation steps between episodes. Requires episodic metadata (counts, return estimates) and a mechanism for structural abstraction (clustering, pattern mining).
special_cases: Single highly valuable one-off episodes may be preserved in ES but may not be consolidated if recurrence is absent; however, an override can consolidate if estimated utility is extremely high.
Noisy environments with spurious recurrence can create misleading semantic nodes; require statistical significance filtering or human-in-the-loop validation.

supporting_evidence.text: Neuroscience evidence of offline replay and selective consolidation of memories with behavioral relevance.
ML approaches that mine frequent patterns or cluster episodic traces can produce reusable subroutines/abstractions improving generalization.
Experience replay and offline RL use aggregated statistics across experiences to update global policies and value functions—consolidation mirrors this aggregation for memory abstraction.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Consolidation ideas (replay, clustering frequent patterns) exist in neuroscience and ML.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Explicit dual-threshold (utility + recurrence) consolidation rule with provenance linking for LM agents to ensure traceability and replay-based reification of semantic nodes.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Translates abstract consolidation concepts into actionable rules for LM-based agents operating on textual episodic traces.
references: McClelland et al. (1995) Complementary Learning Systems [consolidation/replay]
Sutton & Barto (2018) Reinforcement Learning [aggregating experiences to learn value]
Ravula et al. (2021) Schema induction works [pattern abstraction]

evaluation.explanation: The underlying neuroscience principles (selective consolidation, replay) and ML concepts (experience replay, pattern mining) are well-established and accurately cited. The theory makes plausible analogies but is proposing a specific design for LM agents rather than stating proven facts about them. No direct factual contradictions, but the dual-threshold mechanism itself hasn't been empirically verified in this specific form for LM agents.

The theory provides concrete, measurable criteria: utility threshold U_thresh, minimum recurrence k_min, specific timing (offline/consolidation phases), required metadata (episode counts, return estimates), and explicit mechanisms (provenance pointers, structural abstraction via clustering/pattern mining). Variables and conditions are well-defined and operationalizable.

The theory explicitly acknowledges existing consolidation concepts from neuroscience and replay/pattern-mining from ML. The novel contribution is the specific dual-threshold rule (utility AND recurrence) with provenance linking, tailored for LM agents on textual episodes. This represents a moderately novel synthesis and instantiation rather than a fundamentally new principle.

The theory makes clear falsifiable predictions: episodes meeting both thresholds should consolidate while others don't, consolidated nodes should improve generalization on structurally similar tasks, and dual-threshold should outperform single-criterion approaches. Specific experiments comparing consolidation strategies, measuring generalization performance, and evaluating provenance utility are straightforward to design and execute.

The mechanism strongly aligns with established neuroscience principles (selective consolidation based on behavioral relevance, systems consolidation theory) and ML practices (experience replay, pattern abstraction). The dual-threshold approach is sensible for balancing utility and generalizability, and provenance tracking is computationally feasible. No conflicts with known principles of memory or learning systems.

While the theory draws on well-tested neuroscience and RL principles, this specific dual-threshold consolidation rule with provenance linking for LM agents appears to be a design proposal without direct empirical validation. The supporting evidence concerns related principles (replay, pattern mining) rather than tests of this particular mechanism in LM-based agent architectures.

law.theory_description: Agents augment transformer language models with a compact, learned predictive transition model stored in memory (a world-model memory). This memory stores generalized transition primitives (compressed state-action -> predicted next-observation / latent_delta) that an LM can query to simulate outcomes, enabling multi-step lookahead, counterfactual reasoning, and sample-efficient planning in text games without full environment interaction.

law.theory_statement: Given predictive memory M with per-step prediction error e (expected distance between predicted and true next observation in an observation-similarity metric), the expected error of an n-step simulated rollout grows approximately as E_rollout(n) = O(n^p * e) with p in [0.5,1] in typical structured text games (p<1 because symbolic constraints and canonical outcomes correct cumulative drift); the agent's effective planning depth is the largest n such that E_rollout(n) remains below a task-dependent planning-threshold T_plan.
law.domain_scope: Text games with structural constraints (objects, canonical verbs, deterministic or low-entropy outcomes) where an observation-similarity metric (e.g., semantic embedding distance or token-overlap) meaningfully correlates with downstream decision quality.
special_cases: If the game contains branching hidden state not recoverable from text outputs, error may grow linearly or faster (p≈1).
If memory M includes symbolic constraints or invariants (e.g., object-ownership algebra), p can be <0.5 for some environments.

supporting_evidence.text: Empirical work on learned simulators shows cumulative model error grows with rollout length; however, structural priors and symbolic constraints can mitigate drift.
Many text games enforce invariants (e.g., an object cannot be in two places simultaneously), which can be exploited by a learned model to reduce error growth.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Model-based RL analyses of compounding model error in rollouts are established; world-model work formalizes error accumulation.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Applying an explicit approximate scaling law for rollout error in text-game language-space and connecting it to an LM's effective planning depth via a task threshold T_plan.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Extends existing model-error analyses into the textual / symbolic domain and makes testable predictions about planning depth.
references: Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) 'World Models' [model-based rollouts, error accumulation]
Côté et al. (2018) 'TextWorld' [structured text-game mechanics]

evaluation.explanation: The O(n^p * e) formulation is plausible, but the specific claim that p < 1 due to symbolic constraints 'correcting drift' is weakly supported. Standard model error theory suggests errors compound without active correction; constraints may bound but not reduce cumulative error. The p=0.5 case (random walk) is established, but attributing p ∈ [0.5,1] specifically to constraint-based correction oversimplifies error dynamics in learned models.

The theory provides concrete quantitative predictions: explicit error scaling O(n^p * e), bounded exponent range [0.5,1], and operationalizable concepts (observation-similarity metric, planning threshold T_plan). The domain scope is clearly defined. However, how symbolic constraints mechanistically produce p < 1 lacks detailed specification.

The application of rollout error scaling laws to text-game world models is a reasonable domain extension. However, the core ideas (compound model error, planning depth limitations, symbolic structure reducing uncertainty) are well-established in model-based RL and world models literature. The synthesis is incremental rather than fundamentally novel.

The theory makes clear, falsifiable quantitative predictions: measure per-step error e, perform n-step rollouts, fit the exponent p, and verify it falls in [0.5,1]. The relationship between E_rollout(n) and planning success at threshold T_plan is empirically testable in text games. Multiple measurement approaches (semantic distance, token overlap) are suggested.

The overall framework is plausible: learned models do accumulate error over rollouts, and text games have structural regularities. The p=0.5 boundary (random walk) is mechanistically sound. However, the claim that symbolic constraints actively correct drift to produce p < 1 lacks clear mechanistic justification—constraints typically bound rather than reverse error accumulation. The theory is broadly consistent with known behavior but overclaims the corrective mechanism.

While world models and model-based RL have been extensively tested, this specific scaling law for text-game rollouts appears untested. The theory references general empirical work on learned simulators but provides no evidence of direct measurement of p in text games or validation of the constraint-correction mechanism. The specific quantitative predictions (p ∈ [0.5,1] in structured text games) lack empirical demonstration.

raw_results.response_text:
I'll evaluate this theory systematically using established scientific knowledge from model-based RL, world models, and text game research.

## Analysis

**Theory Summary**: The Rollout-Query Law posits that prediction error in multi-step rollouts from a learned world model grows as O(n^p * e) where p ∈ [0.5,1], with p < 1 due to symbolic constraints that correct drift in structured text games.

**Key Claims to Evaluate**:
1. Error growth follows O(n^p * e) scaling
2. p ∈ [0.5,1] in typical structured text games
3. p < 1 because symbolic constraints correct cumulative drift
4. Planning depth is determined by when E_rollout(n) exceeds T_plan

**Known Scientific Context**:
- Standard model-based RL: Unconstrained learned models typically exhibit linear or worse error accumulation (compound error)
- World Models (Ha & Schmidhuber 2018): Demonstrates cumulative drift in learned forward models
- Text games (TextWorld, Jericho): Have deterministic state transitions and structural constraints
- General model error theory: Without corrective mechanisms, errors typically compound multiplicatively or linearly

The claim that p < 1 specifically *because* symbolic constraints correct drift is questionable. While symbolic constraints can reduce some errors, the mathematical form O(n^p * e) with p < 1 (sublinear growth in steps) is unusual for sequential prediction without explicit error correction mechanisms. Standard analysis suggests:
- Independent errors: O(√n) growth (p=0.5) - random walk
- Correlated/compound errors: O(n) or worse (p≥1)
- Sublinear p < 0.5 requires strong correction mechanisms

The theory's claim that p ∈ [0.5,1] is plausible if we interpret this as spanning from random-walk behavior to linear accumulation, but the attribution to symbolic constraints "correcting cumulative drift" lacks clear mechanistic support. Constraints might *bound* errors but don't typically provide active correction.

**Testability**: The theory makes quantitative predictions about error growth that could be measured empirically in text games.

**Novelty**: Applying scaling law analysis to text-game rollouts is a reasonable extension, though the underlying concepts are well-established.

law.theory_description: LM agents should maintain a structured memory with two cooperative layers: (1) episodic traces (detailed transcripts of recent interactions) and (2) compressed semantic abstractions (generalized facts, rules, object affordances). Together these layers enable both one-shot recovery from surprises and transfer across similar games.

law.domain_scope: Text games and interactive text environments where repeated patterns occur across episodes (multiple runs or different rooms/scenarios) and where transcripts can be aligned into templated event structures.
special_cases: Singleton events with high importance (rare but critical) may be missed by frequency-based compression and require dedicated attention mechanisms.
Spurious co-occurrences due to coincidental language patterns may produce incorrect abstractions if not validated.

Engineering systems (e.g., learning from demonstrations and pattern mining) successfully extract rules from repeated trajectories.

law.theory_description: A unified, differentiable control policy for episodic key-value memory in language-model (LM) agents playing text games. UDDMC gates writes based on probabilistic surprise multiplied by an estimator of expected future utility, modulated by an explicit storage cost; it controls retrieval concentration via a learned mapping from epistemic uncertainty to softmax temperature; and it implements end-to-end differentiable reads/writes so reward gradients backpropagate to memory-addressing and content. The result is sparse, high-utility memory contents, focused retrieval when the agent is uncertain, and improved long-horizon credit assignment for textual decision sequences.

law.theory_statement: Write o_t to episodic key-value memory iff Surprise(o_t) * EstimatedExpectedUtilityGain(o_t) > lambda * StorageCost, where Surprise(o_t) = -log p_model(o_t | context); EstimatedExpectedUtilityGain is a learned (or bootstrapped) estimate of the marginal increase in future return enabled by retaining o_t; lambda is a resource-cost scalar. The write operation is implemented as a differentiable gating scalar in [0,1] (soft write) so gradients from downstream rewards can update both content encodings and the utility estimator.
law.domain_scope: Applies to transformer-based LM agents interacting with text-based interactive environments (e.g., TextWorld/Jericho-style games) where (1) observations are token sequences, (2) memory capacity or per-step retrieval token budget is constrained, and (3) the agent is trained with gradient-based methods (supervised fine-tuning and/or policy gradients). Not intended for purely symbolic planners without learnable likelihood models, nor for settings where observation likelihoods are unavailable or meaningless (e.g., adversarial token streams without a trainable model).
special_cases: Purely random environments: Surprise is high but EstimatedExpectedUtilityGain is near-zero; the multiplicative rule prevents wasteful writes.
Zero or negligible storage cost (lambda -> 0): gating collapses toward frequent writes (degenerates to write-every-step policy).
When the utility estimator is poorly trained early in learning, use curriculum (bootstrapped TD targets or auxiliary supervised labels) or conservative thresholds to avoid pathological writes.
Discrete write constraints (hard capacity) can be approximated by soft gates + periodic hard pruning based on learned importance.

supporting_evidence.text: Neuroscience: novelty/surprise signals bias hippocampal encoding and long-term consolidation (novelty-triggered memory formation).
Reinforcement learning: prioritized experience replay stores and samples transitions by priority (e.g., TD-error) to focus learning on high-utility experiences (Schaul et al. 2015).
Episodic control: model-free episodic caches store high-return episodes for rapid retrieval (Blundell et al. 2016).
Likelihood-based novelty measures are commonly available from LMs (next-token probabilities) and have been used to detect surprising inputs.
Complementary learning systems theory: consolidation transfers episodic traces into more stable representations when they are behaviorally significant (McClelland et al. 1995).
Practical LM + memory systems (RAG, RETRO) demonstrate gains from selective retrieval and storage of relevant text chunks, suggesting write selection matters for performance under retrieval budgets.
Temporal-difference/value estimation provides standard mechanisms to estimate marginal utility of stored experiences (Sutton & Barto).

The theory specifies concrete operationalizable components: Surprise(o_t) = -log p_model(o_t | context) is precisely defined using LM likelihoods; the multiplicative gate formula with lambda storage-cost scalar is explicit; soft-write gates in [0,1] are clear differentiable operations. The theory synthesizes known concepts—surprise/novelty signals from neuroscience, prioritized replay from RL, differentiable memory from neural architectures, and LM likelihood as a surprise proxy—into a unified, differentiable gating rule for text-game LM agents. The multiplicative gate combining probabilistic surprise with learned utility, scaled by explicit storage cost, and integrated end-to-end with transformers is a moderately novel contribution. It is not a radically new principle (components exist separately in literature), but the specific combination, operationalization, and tight integration with LM agents in text games represents a non-trivial synthesis. law.theory_description: Agents that play text games using language models are best served by a hierarchical memory architecture that separates (1) detailed episodic traces (raw transcripts, recent observations, exact action sequences), (2) compressed schemas/semantic summaries (location types, object affordances, quest templates), and (3) prospective (intention/subgoal) memory holding short conditional plans. The hierarchy trades off recoverable detail for generalization and supports efficient retrieval, long-horizon credit assignment, and reuse of strategies under partial observability and limited retrieval bandwidth.

law.theory_statement: For LM-based agents in text games with constrained memory-retrieval bandwidth, organizing memory into at least two levels — detailed episodic traces E and compressed schemas S — yields higher task performance P for a fixed total token/storage budget than a flat memory representation. Performance is approximately a monotonic function of an effective-memory utility M_eff = α|S| + β|E| where α > β > 0 (α and β are per-unit utility weights reflecting schema generality vs episodic specificity). When tasks demand long-horizon reasoning and recurring structures, marginal utility per unit of S exceeds that of E.
law.domain_scope: Applies to agents whose controller is an autoregressive language model (transformer-family or similar) that (a) generates actions as text tokens, (b) experiences partially observable, language-described states (interactive fiction / text-adventure / TextWorld / Jericho-style environments), (c) operates under bounded per-step context/token budgets (limited retrieval/conditioning tokens), and (d) faces tasks requiring multi-step strategies across tens-to-hundreds of steps with recurring substructures.
special_cases: Environments with effectively no recurrence or shared structure (high-entropy stochastic descriptions) reduce the value of S; α ≈ β.
Extremely short episodes (≤ ~5 steps) or single-shot tasks where no generalization is useful make hierarchical indexing overhead detrimental.
When memory indexing/retrieval is near-perfect (very high precision), raw episodic capacity E can approach the utility of compressed schemas.

supporting_evidence.text: Memory-augmented neural architectures (Memory Networks, DNC) separate stored traces and retrieved content, improving QA and sequential tasks.

Text game benchmarks (TextWorld, Jericho) and analyses show recurring room types, object affordances, and repeatable quest/recipe patterns that are amenable to schema abstraction.

The supporting facts are accurate, but the quantitative formulation extrapolates beyond established findings.

The theory provides concrete components (episodic traces E, schemas S, prospective memory), a quantitative utility function, and identifies key variables (α, β, |S|, |E|, P). However, critical operational details are underspecified: how to measure |S| and |E| (tokens? entries? semantic units?), how schemas are extracted/compressed, and how retrieval bandwidth constraints translate to the model. The theory synthesizes well-established ideas (episodic/semantic memory from cognitive science, retrieval-augmented LMs, hierarchical RL abstractions) and applies them to LM-based text-game agents. The novelty lies in the explicit quantitative decomposition M_eff = α|S| + β|E| and the prescriptive claim α > β for this specific domain. This represents a moderate reframing and formalization of known principles rather than a fundamentally new mechanism.

The theory makes clear falsifiable predictions: performance P should increase with hierarchical memory over flat memory for fixed budget, and fitting the utility function should yield α > β in the specified domain. Experiments could systematically vary |S| and |E|, measure task success, and estimate parameters. However, ambiguity in operationalizing memory 'size' and the need for careful control of confounds (retrieval mechanisms, indexing quality) moderately complicate clean falsification.

The mechanism is broadly plausible and aligns with established principles: hierarchical memory architectures are neurobiologically inspired, retrieval efficiency benefits from indexing, and abstraction aids generalization in RL. The linear additive utility model is a reasonable first-order approximation, though real cognitive and computational systems typically exhibit non-linear interactions. The claim α > β is intuitive for tasks with recurring structure but may not hold universally even within the stated scope (e.g., highly varied, low-structure text games).

While the general principles (retrieval helps, hierarchy helps, abstraction aids long-horizon tasks) are well-supported across cognitive science and AI, the specific quantitative law M_eff = α|S| + β|E| with α > β has not been directly tested in the stated domain. The theory extrapolates from related findings rather than resting on direct empirical validation.

law.theory_name: Hierarchical Episodic-Semantic Memory (HESM) for Language-Model Agents
law.theory_description: A structured memory architecture that separates short-term working memory (recent tokens and actions), an episodic store of sparse, time-stamped experiences, and a semantic graph (grounded facts and abstractions). HESM prescribes consolidation rules (when and how episodic traces are abstracted into semantic graph nodes), retrieval arbitration across levels based on task-context and utility, and grounded alignment between textual episodes and semantic nodes so that LMs can generalize past episodic outcomes to novel but structurally similar quests.

law.theory_statement: At query-time, retrieval arbitration scores candidate items from each tier using a utility-weighted combination: Score = alpha(context_relevance) * Beta_tier * Importance, where Beta_tier encodes preferred tier given task horizon (e.g., WM high for immediate next-token prediction; ES preferred for episodic recall; SG favored for generalized planning); a learned gating network chooses alpha and Beta_tier conditioned on LM state and uncertainty.
law.domain_scope: Applies when multiple tiers are present and retrieval budgets require choosing which tier to draw from. The arbitration mechanism must be differentiable when end-to-end training is used.
special_cases: When WM contains sufficient tokens, arbitration can defer to WM and ignore ES/SG to save retrieval tokens.
When SG contains conflicting or outdated facts, arbitration must prefer recent ES traces or use provenance for disambiguation.

supporting_evidence.text: Hybrid memory systems in AI often require a controller to decide which memory to consult based on context and cost (e.g., gating in hierarchical caches).
