The core idea of using macro-actions for hierarchical planning is well-established in RL literature (options framework, HAM, MAXQ). The novelty lies in the specific application to text-game agents with explicit numeric thresholds and a direct controlled comparison against compressed-embedding memory under equal memory budgets. This represents a moderately novel synthesis that combines known hierarchical RL concepts with memory-constrained text-game planning, adding quantitative boundary conditions not typically specified in prior work.

The theory is highly testable with a fully specified discriminating experiment: constructing procedurally-generated games with controlled subroutine frequency (25%), implementing three distinct agent architectures with identical decision policies and memory budgets, and measuring success rates across 40 games × 30 seeds. The falsification criterion is crisp (≥7 percentage point difference in mean success, p<0.05). The experimental design is feasible with existing text-game frameworks and requires no novel instrumentation, making independent replication straightforward.

The mechanism is strongly plausible and well-aligned with established hierarchical RL principles showing that temporal abstraction reduces effective horizon and improves sample efficiency in structured environments. The claim that compression of ≥20% of steps via macros yields planning benefits is consistent with known results on chunking and hierarchical planning. Minor uncertainty exists around whether the specific numeric thresholds (k≥4, B≥8) represent true phase boundaries versus continuous gradients, and whether online macro discovery overhead might offset benefits in some regimes, but the core mechanism is sound.

The theory appears to be primarily a predictive framework rather than a summary of completed experiments. While hierarchical RL and macro-actions have extensive empirical support in general RL domains, the specific claims about text-game agents with these exact numeric thresholds, memory budgets, and comparison to compressed-embedding approaches have minimal direct empirical coverage in published literature. The discriminating experiment is proposed but there is no indication it has been executed or that the precise predictions have been tested.

law.theory_name: Sparse Landmark Anchoring and Low-frequency Replay (SLARF)
law.theory_description: Store only high-surprise 'landmark' events (top P percentile) and perform low-frequency scheduled replay; this sparse-anchor + low-replay regime enables efficient long-horizon planning by preserving salient connectors while avoiding retrieval noise from dense stores.

law.statement_name: Sparsity-replay fork: landmarks with low-frequency replay vs dense high-frequency replay
law.theory_statement: Fork: For text-game agents solving long-horizon tasks (episode length >=500) the regime 'store only top P=5% surprise events + replay frequency F = 0.02 per step' yields higher planning efficiency than 'store P >=20% + replay F >= 0.2 per step'; numeric thresholds: P=5% and F=0.02; negative prediction: increasing storage density to P >=20% and replay frequency to F >=0.2 will NOT increase planning efficiency and will reduce planning success per unit compute by >=12% relative to the sparse regime; discriminating_experiment: run agents with storage percentile P in {1%,5%,20%,50%} and replay frequency F in {0.00,0.02,0.2,0.5}; evaluate planning success, average retrieval latency, and planning-success-per-compute on 80 long-horizon games (episode length 500-1200) requiring bridging sparse connectors; falsifies_if: if any dense-high-replay configuration (P>=20% and F>=0.2) yields >=10% higher planning-success-per-compute than P=5%,F=0.02, the statement is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Text-adventure games with sparse salient connectors (critical events occur <5% of time), episode lengths >=500 steps, and agents constrained by limited retrieval compute budget per step.
special_cases: Games with uniformly informative steps where 'surprise' distribution is flat; landmark selection fails.
Environments where replay is free (no compute budget), making high-frequency replay viable.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Storing and replaying only high-surprise events reduces noise and focuses learning/planning on connectors between sparse rewards.
inference: High-frequency replay over dense stores amplifies spurious correlations and increases retrieval cost per useful planning step, lowering planning efficiency.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Experience replay and prioritized replay are known in RL; sparse episodic landmarks are discussed in cognitive literature.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Concrete sparse threshold P=5% combined with low-frequency replay F=0.02 and an explicit performance penalty for denser/higher-frequency regimes, forming a falsifiable fork.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Combines percentile-based sparsity and absolute replay-frequency thresholds to create a discriminable experimental claim.

law.discriminating_experiment: Implement an LM-agent with identical surprise metric. Sweep P in {1,5,20,50}% and F in {0,0.02,0.2,0.5}. Run on 80 long-horizon procedurally generated games (500-1200 steps) needing sparse connectors. Measure planning-success-per-compute and retrieval latency.
law.falsifies_if: Any configuration with P>=20% and F>=0.2 achieves >=10% higher planning-success-per-compute than P=5%,F=0.02 across the suite (p<0.05).

evaluation.explanation: The core principles align with established findings: prioritized experience replay improves sample efficiency in RL (Schaul et al., 2015), and sparse high-salience memory is computationally efficient in cognitive models. However, the specific numeric thresholds (P=5%, F=0.02) and the claimed 12% performance penalty are not empirically validated in published literature. The directional claims are consistent with known mechanisms, but the precise quantitative predictions remain unverified.

The theory provides concrete numeric thresholds (P=5%, F=0.02), explicit experimental parameters (episode lengths 500-1200, 80 games, specific P and F sweep values), clear metrics (planning-success-per-compute, retrieval latency), and quantitative falsification criteria (>=10% improvement threshold, p<0.05). The surprise metric for landmark selection and the compute budget constraint are operationally defined, making the theory highly actionable.

Prioritized experience replay based on temporal-difference error or surprise is well-established in RL (e.g., prioritized replay buffers). Sparse episodic memory aligned with salience is familiar from cognitive science and event segmentation theory. The novelty lies in the specific quantitative combination of sparse storage percentiles with low replay frequency as an efficiency regime, and the explicit fork prediction contrasting it against dense-high-frequency alternatives. This represents a moderate recombination rather than a fundamentally new principle.

The theory specifies a clear, feasible experimental protocol: sweep discrete parameter values (P in {1,5,20,50}%, F in {0,0.02,0.2,0.5}), apply to a defined domain (text-adventure games with 500-1200 step episodes), measure objective metrics (planning success, latency, success-per-compute), and provides quantitative falsification criteria (>=10% improvement at p<0.05). Implementation is straightforward given existing RL frameworks and text-game environments, with no requirement for exotic apparatus or measurements.

The mechanism is plausible and aligns with known computational trade-offs: storing only high-surprise events reduces memory noise and retrieval cost, which is consistent with information-theoretic principles and findings from prioritized replay. Low-frequency replay avoiding overfitting to spurious patterns is mechanistically reasonable. However, the specific claim that P=5%/F=0.02 uniformly dominates denser regimes across all long-horizon sparse-connector games is strong; performance may depend on game-specific structure, surprise metric quality, and whether landmarks adequately cover state space for effective planning.

To my knowledge, no published studies have directly tested this specific combination of sparse percentile-based landmark storage (P=5%) with low-frequency replay (F=0.02) against the proposed dense alternatives in long-horizon text-game planning with compute budget constraints. While general prioritized replay has extensive empirical support in other RL contexts, the particular regime and domain specified here appear untested in the literature.

law.theory_description: LM agents should maintain a structured memory with two cooperative layers: (1) episodic traces (detailed transcripts of recent interactions) and (2) compressed semantic abstractions (generalized facts, rules, object affordances). Retrieval policies mediate between them: episodic retrieval for exact replays and recovery, semantic retrieval for generalization and planning. Together these layers enable both one-shot recovery from surprises and transfer across similar games.

law.theory_statement: At decision time, the agent chooses retrieval from episodic memory when the uncertainty over current state U_state (measured by entropy over plausible states) exceeds a threshold U_e; otherwise it prefers semantic memory retrieval. This balances exact recovery (episodic) and generalization (semantic) and minimizes expected decision-time error.
law.domain_scope: Applies to transformer LM agents with access to both detailed transcripts and compressed semantic facts, where a computational budget for retrieval is constrained and uncertainty estimates are available (e.g., via LM calibration or ensemble spreads).
special_cases: If uncertainty estimates are unreliable, retrieval decisions may misroute, causing failures.
When episodic memory is too large to search efficiently, a pre-filtering or indexing step is required.

supporting_evidence.text: Episodic memory improves recovery of rare events; semantic memory supports generalization. Hybrid retrieval strategies have been effective in other agent contexts.
Uncertainty-based retrieval (querying detailed logs when uncertain) is an established heuristic in decision systems and active learning.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Hybrid memory architectures and uncertainty-driven querying exist in RL and question-answering systems.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Formalizing a decision rule that switches between episodic and semantic memory retrieval based on a measurable current-state uncertainty metric for LM agents in text games.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Synthesis of existing memory ideas into a clear retrieval policy suited for LMs acting in textual environments.
references: Blundell et al. (2016) 'Model-Free Episodic Control' [episodic value storage]
Graves et al. (2016) 'Differentiable Neural Computer' [memories accessed by controller]

evaluation.explanation: The core principles align with established cognitive science (episodic vs. semantic memory) and computational work (episodic control, uncertainty-driven querying). The claim that uncertainty-based switching improves performance is plausible and consistent with active learning principles. However, the specific assertion that this rule 'minimizes expected decision-time error' for LM agents lacks direct empirical verification, making it somewhat speculative though scientifically reasonable.

The rule identifies key variables (state uncertainty U_state via entropy, threshold U_e, retrieval choice) and provides a clear decision criterion. However, critical operational details are underspecified: how to compute entropy over 'plausible states' from LM outputs, how to calibrate U_e, and how to measure 'expected decision-time error.' The framework is concrete enough to guide implementation but needs substantial operationalization work.

The episodic-semantic distinction is classical (Tulving), and uncertainty-driven retrieval exists in active learning and meta-RL. The cited works (DNC, episodic control) demonstrate hybrid memory architectures. The contribution is formalizing a specific entropy-threshold switching rule for LM agents in text environments—a reasonable synthesis and application of existing principles to a specific domain rather than a fundamental innovation.

The theory makes testable predictions: implementing the dual memory system with entropy-based switching should outperform single-memory baselines or alternative switching rules. Experiments could vary threshold U_e, measure task performance, and test recovery vs. generalization tradeoffs. The domain (LM agents in text games) is well-defined. The main limitation is that 'expected decision-time error' needs precise operationalization, but multiple falsifiable experiments are feasible.

The mechanism is highly plausible and well-grounded in both cognitive science (complementary memory systems) and computational principles (explore/exploit, active learning). Using uncertainty to trigger detailed episodic retrieval when the agent is uncertain, versus compressed semantic knowledge when confident, aligns with established decision-making heuristics. Entropy as an uncertainty proxy is standard in ML. The mechanism coheres well with known principles of adaptive information retrieval.

While the component concepts (episodic buffers, semantic compression, LM uncertainty) have been studied separately, this specific entropy-threshold switching rule for LM agents appears untested. The cited references support the general memory paradigm but don't evaluate this particular decision rule. The theory appears to be a proposal rather than an empirically validated mechanism, with minimal direct experimental support for its core claims.

law.theory_description: Agents should allocate a fixed number of high-priority memory 'goal slots' separate from a larger episodic store; above a concrete slot-count threshold these slots enable chaining of subgoals and reduce interference, improving long-horizon quest completion at the cost of some short-term reactivity.

law.statement_name: Slot-count fork: prioritized slots vs recency-only
law.theory_statement: Fork: For text-game agents with total memory capacity M_total and episodic event-level entries, configuring K prioritized goal slots with K >= 128 improves long-horizon quest-completion rate compared to K <= 32; numeric threshold: K = 128 slots; negative prediction: setting K <= 32 will NOT improve long-horizon quest-completion (it will reduce completion rate by >=10% relative to K=128) for games with required plan-chain length >=3 and episode length >=300; discriminating_experiment: keep M_total constant, implement identical retrieval and scoring mechanisms, vary K in {16,32,128,512} and evaluate on 100 procedurally generated quests requiring plan-chain length 3-7 and episode length 300-800; measure quest-completion rate and average subgoal chaining length; falsifies_if: if K <= 32 yields >=10% higher quest-completion rate than K=128 across the evaluation suite (p<0.05).
law.domain_scope: Text-adventure games where (a) events are discrete steps (actions/observations) stored as entries, (b) tasks require chaining >=3 subgoals, and (c) total memory capacity M_total is fixed between 8k and 64k entries.
special_cases: Purely reactive micro-games with plan-chain length = 0 where prioritized slots are unnecessary.
Extremely long-world-knowledge tasks where relevant facts are immutable and should be stored in a separate persistent KB rather than goal slots.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Fixed dedicated high-priority slots reduce interference between temporally local episodic traces and stable task-critical goals by creating a separate address space for high-utility items.

inference: When K is too small relative to required concurrent subgoals, goal slot eviction causes loss of intermediate targets and breaks chaining; increasing K above a threshold reinstates chaining capability.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Memory prioritization and dedicated buffers are used in some agents and cognitive models, but fixed numeric slot-count thresholds are rarely specified.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: An explicit fork committing that K >= 128 is necessary (vs K <= 32) for improved long-horizon chaining under constrained total capacity, producing a falsifiable numeric bound and prediction of a >=10% effect.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Goes beyond general prioritization by prescribing slot-count and a discriminating experiment.

law.discriminating_experiment: Implement an LM-based agent with identical scoring and retrieval; set total store size to 16k event entries. Create four variants with K=16,32,128,512 dedicated goal slots. Generate 100 procedural quests requiring 3-7 chained subgoals and episode lengths 300-800. Evaluate quest completion and average intact subgoal chains.
law.falsifies_if: Any K <= 32 yields >=10% higher quest-completion rate than K=128 across the test suite (p<0.05).

evaluation.explanation: The theory's central claim that a specific slot count threshold (K=128 vs K≤32) determines long-horizon performance lacks empirical grounding in established AI/RL literature. While memory architectures with prioritization exist (e.g., Neural Turing Machines, Differentiable Neural Computers), the specific numeric threshold of 128 slots is arbitrary and unsupported by known results. The claim that K≤32 will reduce completion by ≥10% is a precise quantitative prediction without corresponding established evidence. The underlying principle of reducing interference through dedicated slots has some basis in working memory models, but the specific parameterization appears speculative.

The theory is highly operationalized with concrete numeric thresholds (K=128 vs K≤32), specific experimental parameters (100 quests, plan-chain length 3-7, episode length 300-800), measurable outcomes (quest-completion rate, subgoal chaining length), and clear statistical criteria (≥10% difference, p<0.05). The domain scope is well-defined (text-adventure games with discrete events, fixed memory capacity 8k-64k entries). Special cases are explicitly enumerated, making the theory readily implementable and testable.

The concept of prioritized memory slots draws on established ideas from working memory models in cognitive science and attention mechanisms in neural architectures. However, the specific contribution—prescribing an exact numeric threshold (K=128) for goal slots with a falsifiable prediction of ≥10% performance difference in long-horizon planning—represents a moderately novel synthesis. The theory goes beyond general architectural principles by committing to a concrete, testable numeric bound rather than merely proposing qualitative prioritization.

The theory provides exceptional testability with explicit experimental design: vary K in {16,32,128,512}, use 100 procedurally generated quests with specified complexity (3-7 subgoals, 300-800 steps), measure quest-completion rate and subgoal chaining, and apply clear falsification criteria (K≤32 outperforming K=128 by ≥10% at p<0.05). The discriminating experiment is feasible, well-specified, and would definitively falsify the theory if results contradict predictions. Multiple measurement axes (completion rate, chaining length) enable robust testing.

The general mechanism—that dedicated high-priority slots reduce interference—aligns with established principles from working memory research and attention mechanisms. However, the specific threshold of 128 slots lacks clear mechanistic justification. Why 128 rather than 64 or 256? The choice appears arbitrary given that plan-chain requirements are 3-7 subgoals (suggesting far fewer slots needed). The claim that performance sharply changes at this specific boundary is plausible in principle (phase transitions occur in computational systems) but the particular value seems disconnected from the stated task requirements, reducing overall plausibility.

There is no evidence in established AI/RL literature that this specific theory—with its precise slot-count thresholds and predicted performance differences—has been empirically tested. While memory-augmented architectures have been studied, the particular claim about K=128 vs K≤32 producing ≥10% differences in long-horizon text-game quest completion appears entirely untested. The theory is well-designed for testing but currently lacks any empirical validation.

law.theory_description: LM agents should condition memory retrieval on candidate actions: when evaluating an action a, retrieve only those episodic summaries and substructures that are action-relevant (e.g., contain same objects, locations, verbs). Retrieved contexts are then synthesized via the LM into an imagined successor observation distribution. Action-conditioned retrieval reduces noise, improves model-based rollouts, and enables counterfactual evaluation of candidate action sequences without full environment execution.

law.theory_statement: Let p_hat be the LM-conditioned predicted success probability per simulated step for candidate rollouts using retrieved contexts, and c_sim be cost (compute) per simulated step. If p_hat > p_env * (c_env / c_sim) where p_env is baseline empirical success-per-step in environment and c_env is environment interaction cost, then counterfactual evaluation reduces expected real-environment interactions required to find successful plans.
law.domain_scope: Applies to scenarios where simulation cost is meaningfully cheaper than environment interactions, and the LM's synthesized successor distributions produce informative predictions (p_hat reasonably correlated with real outcomes).
special_cases: If synthesized predictions are uncorrelated with environment outcomes, p_hat is unreliable and the inequality fails; counterfactual rollouts then mislead.
For extremely cheap environment steps (e.g., deterministic local simulator), simulation advantage disappears.

supporting_evidence.text: Model-based RL theory shows benefit when model predictions are accurate relative to environment costs; LM-based synthesis acts as a learned model.
Empirical work on planning with learned models shows reduction in environment interactions when model-step accuracy is sufficient.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Model-based RL provides cost-benefit analyses for planning vs. executing; retrieval-augmented LMs have been used for simulation-like tasks.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Applying a concrete inequality binding predicted per-step success probability, simulation vs. environment costs, and LM-action-conditioned retrieval for text-game agents.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This statement formalizes when LM-based counterfactual rollouts help; it adapts established model-based cost bounds to LM-retrieval synthesis in text games.
references: Ha & Schmidhuber (2018) World Models [model-based planning]
Sutton (1991) Dyna Architecture [planning with learned models]
Lewis et al. (2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation [retrieval improves generation]

evaluation.explanation: The inequality structure conflates success probability with prediction accuracy. In proper model-based RL theory, benefit depends on model fidelity (prediction error/correlation), not raw success probabilities. A model with high predicted success (p_hat) could be systematically wrong about which actions work, making the inequality misleading. The general claim that model-based planning can reduce environment interactions is correct, but this specific formulation misrepresents the cost-benefit analysis.

The law provides concrete variables (p_hat, p_env, c_sim, c_env) and a testable inequality. However, p_hat is underspecified - it's unclear whether this is the model's confidence, empirical success rate of model-recommended actions, or something else. The phrase 'success probability per simulated step' is ambiguous about what constitutes success. Despite these gaps, the basic framework is reasonably operationalizable with contextual interpretation.

This represents a moderately novel synthesis of established ideas: model-based RL cost-benefit analysis, retrieval-augmented generation, and action-conditioned memory for LM agents. The specific contribution is formalizing a decision rule for when LM-based counterfactual rollouts are beneficial in text environments. While not groundbreaking, it's a coherent integration that hasn't been explicitly formalized this way in prior work.

The inequality provides clear falsifiable predictions: measure p_hat and p_env empirically, measure costs c_sim and c_env, then verify whether satisfying the inequality correlates with reduced real environment interactions. Multiple test scenarios are feasible across different cost ratios and LM capabilities. The main limitation is ambiguity in p_hat measurement, but reasonable operationalizations exist.

The core mechanism (action-conditioned retrieval enabling useful counterfactual planning) is plausible and aligns with model-based RL principles. However, the specific mathematical formulation is problematic. In established model-based planning theory, benefit depends on prediction accuracy/fidelity, not raw success probability ratios. The inequality could hold vacuously (high p_hat from overconfident predictions) while providing no actual benefit. A formulation based on prediction error would be more mechanistically sound.

While there is work on planning with LMs and retrieval-augmented generation, I am not aware of experiments specifically testing this cost-benefit inequality for LM-based counterfactual rollouts. Existing work on LM planning (SayCan, Reflexion, etc.) typically doesn't measure the specific trade-off formalized here. The particular claim about when this inequality predicts reduced environment interactions appears largely untested in available literature.

law.theory_description: For long-horizon, low-stochasticity text-adventure games requiring deep planning, a sparse key-value episodic memory with constrained capacity and narrow retrieval (top-k small) yields higher task success than extending the transformer's dense attention context beyond practical limits. The mechanism is reduced attention noise and preserved salient episodic structure through compact indexed retrieval.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For deterministic or low-stochasticity text games with required planning depth T_planning >= 500 steps, an episodic-indexing memory (key-value store capacity C in [64,512], top-k retrieval k = 8) will outperform extending the LM dense attention context window beyond W = 2048 tokens. Numeric thresholds: T_planning >= 500, W_threshold = 2048 tokens, episodic capacity C ∈ [64,512], top-k k = 8. Negative prediction: Increasing the dense attention window to W >= 8192 tokens will NOT increase mean success rate relative to the episodic-indexing agent and will reduce success if episodic capacity C < 64 or top-k k > 32.
law.domain_scope: Deterministic or low-stochasticity (per-step transition entropy < 1.0 nat) single-player text-adventure games with required planning depth >= 500 steps, branching factor B ∈ [2,6], running on transformer LMs with baseline context capacity 2048 tokens and parameter count between 100M and 10B.
special_cases: Observations whose single-step tokenized length > 2048 tokens (single-observation huge blobs) — episodic keys that summarize such blobs could lose necessary detail and fail.
Environments with per-step transition entropy >= 2.0 nats (high stochasticity) where past episodic traces poorly predict future transitions.
Tasks that require remembering continuous numeric values or counters with fine precision across >10k steps — compact embedding keys may quantize away required precision.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Dense self-attention cost and interference scale with token count; retrieving a small set of salient items from a key-value index avoids quadratic attention to all tokens.

inference: For planning horizons >> context window length, repeated retrieval of temporally-distant salient states via compact indices preserves decision-relevant information without linear growth in attention computation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Existing work proposes both extending transformer context and using external memory; prior comparisons are qualitative or lack concrete numeric cross-over thresholds.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Concrete numeric cross-over claim (planning depth >=500 favors episodic-indexing; context window W>=8192 will not beat episodic-indexing under stated capacity/top-k bounds) and an explicit negative prediction about large context windows degrading performance under capacity constraints.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Extends prior memory-vs-context work by committing to numeric thresholds and a falsifiable comparative experiment.

law.discriminating_experiment: Take a fixed pretrained 1B-parameter transformer LM (no architecture change to model internals). Build two agents: (A) Episodic-indexing with capacity C=256 entries, key dim 128, top-k=8 retrieval, write-on-event novelty policy; (B) Extended-context with no external memory but transformer context window W=8192 tokens (all other training/data equal). Evaluate both on 100 procedurally-generated deterministic text games with average required planning depth >=500 and branching factor ≈3. Metric: mean success rate across games; run 30 random seeds per game. Compare means with paired test.
law.falsifies_if: Agent (B) with extended context W >= 8192 achieves an absolute >10 percentage-point higher mean success rate than agent (A) across the evaluation suite (p < 0.05 by paired test).

evaluation.explanation: Core mechanistic principles (quadratic attention cost, retrieval benefits) are accurate. However, the specific numeric crossover thresholds (planning depth ≥500, W=2048 threshold, C∈[64,512]) and the strong prediction that W≥8192 will underperform are speculative and not established by empirical evidence. Recent work on very long context transformers (100K+ tokens) suggests the negative prediction may be overstated. Mixed accuracy: sound principles, unverified specific claims.

Includes fully operationalized discriminating experiment with clear protocol, metrics, and statistical criteria. Only minor ambiguities prevent a perfect score (e.g., exact definition of 'write-on-event novelty policy').

The episodic memory versus extended context comparison exists in prior literature (memory-augmented neural networks, retrieval-augmented generation). This represents a moderately novel synthesis that makes existing ideas testable through specific empirical commitments.

Highly testable with clear falsification criteria. Implementation is feasible with current technology (procedurally-generated text games, standard transformers). Multiple independent predictions allow testing different aspects (main effect, capacity constraints, top-k sensitivity). Clear and actionable.

Underlying mechanism (quadratic attention cost, retrieval preserving salient information) is well-established and plausible. However, the specific comparative claim that W≥8192 will definitively underperform episodic indexing conflicts with emerging evidence from very long context transformers (e.g., 100K+ token models) that show strong performance on long-horizon tasks. The mechanism is broadly consistent with known principles but the specific numeric predictions may overstate the disadvantage of extended context. Plausible but incomplete.

To my knowledge, this specific comparative hypothesis with these exact parameters has not been empirically tested in published work. While related work exists on memory-augmented RL and long-context transformers separately, the direct head-to-head comparison at these numeric thresholds (planning depth ≥500, episodic C∈[64,512] k=8 vs. context W≥8192) in deterministic text-adventure games appears untested. The theory is minimally tested with only indirect empirical contact from related domains.

law.theory_description: When environment-change events (inventory change, location change, new NPC encounter) occur sparsely relative to steps, restricting writes to event-triggered snapshots yields higher retrieval precision and downstream performance than continuous per-step writes for the same memory budget. The claim sets a concrete event-rate threshold below which event-triggered write strictly dominates continuous write.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For text games where environment-change event rate E_rate <= 0.25 (i.e., <=25% of steps are environment-change events) and average episode length L >= 100, an event-triggered write policy (write only on environment-change events) with deduplication will outperform continuous per-step write under equal memory budget; numeric thresholds: E_rate = 0.25, L = 100 steps; negative prediction: continuous per-step write will NOT outperform event-triggered write under these conditions and will reduce retrieval precision by >=10 percentage points; discriminating experiment: run two agents identical except write policy on a suite of games engineered to have E_rate=0.1 and L=200: (A) event-triggered write plus simple deduplication; (B) continuous per-step write until budget exhausted. Measure retrieval precision and success rate; falsifier: if continuous-write yields >=7% higher retrieval precision or success rate than event-write, the statement is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Text-adventure games where environment-change events are clearly defined and occur in <=25% of steps, average episode length >=100, and memory budgets constrained to small megabytes.
special_cases: Games with noisy or high-frequency event signals where event detection is unreliable; event-triggered policy may drop relevant micro-events.
Tasks where relevant temporal dynamics occur within non-event steps (e.g., a hidden timer that matters even without state-change events).

supporting_evidence.text: Background principle: Writing only salient events reduces redundant storage and focuses retrieval capacity on informative entries under constrained budgets.
Inference: When event_rate is low, continuous writes mostly add redundant near-duplicate states; deduplicated event writes increase effective information per stored entry.

law.theory_description: Transformers implement exact, compositional integer arithmetic by learning low-dimensional linear place-value transforms applied iteratively across layers. There exists a sharp layer-depth-to-digit threshold: when model depth exceeds a numeric multiple of operand digit-length the model uses an algorithmic linear projection + carry propagation mechanism; when depth is below that multiple the model defaults to memorized mapping and fails algorithmic generalization.

law.statement_name: Layer-threshold fork for compositional linear arithmetic
law.theory_statement: Fork: For base-10 integer addition of two D-digit operands, if transformer depth L satisfies L >= 4 * D then Mechanism A (compositional linear arithmetic via learned linear projections and iterative carry propagation across layers) dominates; if L < 4 * D then Mechanism B (sparse memorized mapping of frequent input-output pairs) dominates. Numeric threshold: L = 4 * D. Negative prediction: For L >= 4 * D, held-out exact-addition accuracy on unseen D-digit pairs will NOT fall below 90% after standard training (see domain_scope); for L < 4 * D, held-out exact-addition accuracy will NOT exceed 60% without explicit example memorization of >50% of the input space. Discriminating experiment: For D in {2,4,6}, train transformer models (fixed width 1024, 16 heads) with depths L in {D,2D,3D,4D,5D} on a dataset containing 15% uniformly sampled D-digit addition pairs. Evaluate (1) exact-match accuracy on the held-out 85% of pairs, (2) exact-match accuracy on inputs with digit-place permutation (place-values shuffled), and (3) effect of replacing the final learned linear output projection with a random fixed linear map at inference. If models with L >= 4*D obtain >90% held-out exact accuracy, >85% on permuted-place tests, and drop >50 percentage points when final projection is randomized, Mechanism A is supported; otherwise the fork is false. Falsifier: Observing any model with L >= 4 * D that (a) after adequate training (converged training loss, >100k gradient steps for D>=4) has held-out exact-addition accuracy <= 90% on unseen D-digit pairs, or (b) shows <30 percentage-point accuracy drop when the final learned projection is randomized (indicating lookup-like output encoding), refutes this statement.
law.domain_scope: Decoder-only or encoder-decoder transformer architectures trained from scratch on exact base-10 integer addition of two equal-length operands. Operands are tokenized as single decimal digits (one token per digit), positional encodings present, model width in [512,4096], float16/float32 numerics, training dataset limited to <=20% of the D-digit input space so full memorization is infeasible. No chain-of-thought or explicit carry supervision provided during training. Applies to inference without stepwise prompting.
special_cases: If explicit stepwise/carry supervision is provided during training, the numeric multiplier (4) can reduce by up to 2x (i.e., L >= 2*D suffices).
If tokenization groups k digits per token, effective D is ceil(D/k) and the threshold uses that effective digit-length (see TPVE theory).

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Transformer depth enables multiple sequential transformations of position-wise embeddings; iterative computations can be implemented by repeated layer transforms.

inference: A minimal procedural decomposition of digit-wise addition requires at least four coarse pipeline steps per digit (read digit pair, compute partial sum, propagate carry, write output) motivating the approximately 4*D depth scaling.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior analyses show transformers can implement iterative computations and that depth correlates with algorithmic capacity; work exists on emergent arithmetic-like behaviors without an explicit numeric layer-to-digit rule.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete fork with a precise numeric threshold L = 4 * D, a negative prediction about held-out accuracy, and a single concrete ablation (final linear projection randomization) as a discriminating test between algorithmic and memorized solutions.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Builds on existing qualitative ideas but commits to a falsifiable numeric boundary and concrete experiments.

law.discriminating_experiment: Train transformers (width 1024, 16 heads) on D-digit addition with D in {2,4,6} using 15% uniform coverage of the input space. For each D train models with depths L={D,2D,3D,4D,5D} to convergence. Evaluate exact-match on unseen 85% holdout, exact-match on place-value-permuted inputs, and accuracy after replacing the final learned linear output projection with a fixed random matrix. Support is: L>=4D models have >90% holdout accuracy, >85% permutation accuracy, and >50-point drop after projection randomization. Otherwise falsified.
law.falsifies_if: Any trained model with L >= 4 * D that (a) has held-out exact-match accuracy <= 90% given sufficient training, or (b) shows <30 percentage-point drop in exact-match accuracy when the final learned output projection is randomized, falsifies this statement.

evaluation.explanation: The theory's central quantitative claim—that L >= 4*D is a sharp threshold enabling >90% generalization on unseen addition pairs—is not well-supported by existing empirical work. Studies on transformer arithmetic show that (1) transformers can learn addition but generalization is highly sensitive to representation, training regime, and architecture details, (2) there is no widely established 4*D layer-depth rule in the literature, and (3) many models fail to reach 90% generalization even with sufficient depth unless carefully engineered (e.g., with specific positional encodings or training curricula). The qualitative claim that depth enables iterative computation is accurate, but the precise numeric threshold and performance guarantees appear to be unsupported speculation.

The theory is highly concrete: it specifies exact numeric thresholds (L = 4*D), precise architecture constraints (width 1024, 16 heads, tokenization scheme), training regime (15% coverage, >100k steps), and multiple measurable outcomes (exact-match accuracy on holdout, permuted inputs, and ablation with randomized final projection). law.theory_description: Transformers can implement an associative parallel-prefix (scan) algorithm to compute carries in O(log D) sequential update layers by learning a local associative operator that combines partial-sum + carry information. If the model has depth L exceeding a small constant times log2(D) it will implement PPAS and compute carries in parallel; otherwise it cannot realize PPAS and will either use sequential sweeps or memorize.

law.statement_name: Log-depth fork for associative carry scan
law.theory_statement: Fork: For tokenized D-digit base-10 addition, if model depth L satisfies L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)) then the model will implement a parallel-prefix associative-scan mechanism (Mechanism A dominates) and compute carries in O(log D) sequential layers; if L < 4 * ceil(log2(D)) then PPAS cannot be implemented and the model will not generalize to input lengths > D (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: L = 4 * ceil(log2(D)). Negative prediction: If L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)), accuracy on exact addition will NOT degrade when D is increased up to 2^(L/4) (i.e., models generalize to lengths up to 2^(L/4)) without additional training. Discriminating experiment: Train models with identical width and tokenization on D=128 digit addition with depths L in {16,24,32,48}. For each L, evaluate zero-shot generalization exact-match on D' ∈ {128,256,512,1024}. If models with L >= 4 * ceil(log2(512)) = 4*9 = 36 obtain >90% exact-match on D'=512 and up to 2^(L/4), while models with L < 36 fail to generalize beyond D, PPAS is supported. Falsifier: If models with L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)) systematically fail to generalize to longer D' up to 2^(L/4) (after sufficient training on D) or if successful generalization is only achievable by memorization of specific long examples, PPAS is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Decoder-only or encoder-decoder transformers trained on token-level (single-digit) exact integer addition in base-10; L counts decoder layers that perform inter-position mixing. Applies when model capacity (width) is sufficient to learn an associative binary operator in its layers.
special_cases: If models have insufficient width (e.g., hidden dimension << 128) to represent the associative operator, the L threshold must be increased or PPAS will fail.
If tokenization groups digits (k>1) the effective D should be rescaled by k and the threshold recomputed.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Parallel-prefix (scan) algorithms compute associative aggregates in O(log N) steps by combining adjacent partial results using an associative operator; neural nets can learn associative operators in feedforward layers.

inference: A transformer can realize the associative operator using local layers that combine neighboring positions; repeated doubling (pairwise combination) across ~log2(D) stages suffices to propagate carries globally.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Known connection between associative scan algorithms and parallel architectures; prior work has suggested log-depth algorithmic implementations in neural models but rarely gave explicit depth thresholds tied to D.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Concrete numeric depth threshold L >= 4 * ceil(log2(D)) with a direct prediction of zero-shot generalization range 2^(L/4).
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Makes a risky, testable mapping from transformer depth to maximal zero-shot generalizable input length under a PPAS mechanism.

law.discriminating_experiment: Train transformer decoders (width 1024) on D=128 digit addition with depths L in {16,24,32,48}. After training to convergence on D=128, evaluate exact-match on D' = 256, 512, 1024 without fine-tuning. If models with L >= 36 show >90% exact-match on D'=512 and generalize up to 2^(L/4), and models with L < 36 do not, PPAS is supported.
law.falsifies_if: Models with L >= 4*ceil(log2(D)) fail to achieve >90% exact-match on longer D' up to 2^(L/4) after convergence on D, or if generalization to longer lengths occurs only when those exact longer-length examples were present in training.

evaluation.explanation: While parallel-prefix algorithms exist and transformers can in principle compute associative operations, the specific threshold L ≥ 4×ceil(log₂(D)) lacks empirical support. Established research shows transformers struggle with length generalization on arithmetic even with substantial depth, contradicting the strong generalization claims (>90% accuracy up to 2^(L/4)). The factor of 4 appears unmotivated by known mechanisms.

The theory provides highly concrete predictions: exact numeric threshold (L = 4×ceil(log₂(D))), specific generalization range (2^(L/4)), detailed experimental protocol with specific depths {16,24,32,48}, and quantitative success criterion (>90% exact-match). Variables are well-defined and operationalizable.

The connection between parallel-prefix algorithms and neural networks is known, but the specific quantitative depth threshold tied to generalization range is novel. The precise mapping from architecture (depth L) to zero-shot generalization capability (D' up to 2^(L/4)) represents a substantive and non-trivial mechanistic claim beyond existing work.

Extremely testable with clear falsification criteria. The discriminating experiment specifies exact architectures, training regime, evaluation protocol, and quantitative thresholds. Multiple independent predictions can be tested: the depth threshold, the generalization range formula, and the sharp transition between regimes. Implementation is straightforward and computationally feasible.

The parallel-prefix mechanism is theoretically sound and transformers can represent associative operations. However, the specific threshold and strong generalization claims conflict with established empirical findings showing transformers' poor length generalization on arithmetic. The sharp bifurcation at exactly L = 4×ceil(log₂(D)) seems implausibly precise given the continuous nature of neural network learning and the typical need for architectural inductive biases.

law.theory_description: When training coverage of the mapping from inputs to outputs exceeds a coverage threshold, language models preferentially implement sparse memorized lookup encodings rather than algorithmic compositional arithmetic. This leads to near-perfect accuracy on seen pairs and sharply degraded generalization on unseen pairs; the switch point is a training-coverage numeric threshold independent of depth in the high-capacity regime.

law.statement_name: Coverage fork for memorization vs algorithmic arithmetic
law.theory_statement: Fork: For D-digit base-10 addition, let training coverage C be the fraction of all possible operand pairs seen during training. If C >= 0.25 then Mechanism B (sparse-memory lookup tables stored in embedding/output layers) dominates; if C < 0.25 then Mechanism A (algorithmic compositional arithmetic that generalizes to unseen pairs) dominates. Numeric threshold: C = 0.25 (25%). Negative prediction: For C >= 0.25, held-out exact-addition accuracy on unseen pairs will NOT exceed 55% despite near-perfect accuracy on seen pairs; for C < 0.25, held-out exact-addition accuracy on unseen pairs will NOT be below 85% for models meeting algorithmic capacity (e.g., depth >= 4*D per LLLA). Discriminating experiment: Train identical transformer models (width 1024, 16 heads, depth fixed at 4*D) on D-digit addition with datasets sampled at coverages C ∈ {0.05,0.15,0.25,0.4}. For each C measure: (1) exact-match accuracy on seen training pairs, (2) exact-match accuracy on the held-out unseen pairs, and (3) singular-value spectrum of the final token-output embedding matrix (to detect memorized lookup structure). If C >= 0.25 gives >98% seen accuracy but unseen accuracy <=55% and shows a low-rank plus sparse singular-value pattern consistent with memorized lookup, SMTL is supported. Falsifier: If models trained with C >= 0.25 routinely obtain >85% exact-match accuracy on unseen pairs without signs of sparse lookup embeddings (no large sparse singular values), or if models with C < 0.25 fail to reach >85% unseen accuracy given algorithmic capacity, this statement is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Decoder-only transformers trained from scratch on base-10 D-digit addition, tokenized one digit per token, model depth set at 4*D to ensure algorithmic capacity per LLLA; training data coverage C measured as fraction of all ordered operand pairs seen during training; width between 512 and 4096; no chain-of-thought supervisory signals.
special_cases: If training includes heavy repetition and curriculum that emphasizes rare pairs, memorization threshold C can shift downward (i.e., lower coverage suffices for lookup).
When the model capacity (parameters) is orders of magnitude larger than the mapping cardinality, memorization can occur at lower C.

law.theory_description: Transformer models implement integer addition either by executing an explicit sequential per-digit algorithm across depth (digit-local sequential layers) or by learning a parallel mapping/memorization that mixes digits immediately. The decisive tradeoff is model depth relative to digit-length: when depth L is large enough (>= 6*D) the model organizes computation into per-digit algorithmic steps along depth; when L < 6*D the model implements a parallelized, high-degree cross-position mapping that resembles memorization.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For exact addition of two base-10 integers with digit-length D (D >= 2), if model transformer depth L satisfies L >= 6 * D then the model implements a sequential per-digit algorithm across layers (Mechanism A dominates); if L < 6 * D then the model implements a parallel cross-position mapping/memorization (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: L_threshold = 6 * D. Negative prediction: For models with L >= 6*D, the fraction of attention mass from each token to non-adjacent digit-token positions will NOT exceed 0.15 on average across inference tokens (i.e., non-local attention mass <= 15%). Discriminating_experiment: Train a suite of transformer models with identical parameter counts and width but varying depth L in {3*D, 4*D, 5*D, 6*D, 8*D} on exact base-10 addition (same training data); at test time (held-out examples), (1) measure per-token attention matrices and compute average non-adjacent attention mass, (2) perform layer-ablation: remove/zero each layer i and record which digit positions' outputs degrade when that layer is ablated. DAL predicts that for L >= 6*D (a) non-adjacent attention mass <= 0.15 and (b) ablations will show sequential localized failures consistent with per-digit processing (ablating layer k will mainly damage digit floor(k/6) and higher-order digits processed later). Falsifier: If for models with L >= 6*D the measured non-adjacent attention mass > 0.30 (30%) or layer ablations produce immediate, uniform failure across all digits (no sequential localized pattern), DAL is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Decoder-only or encoder-decoder transformer architectures with fixed tokenization of single decimal digits, trained from scratch on exact base-10 integer addition tasks for digit-lengths D in [2,16]; model width and parameter count kept constant across depth variants; training to >95% exact-addition accuracy on held-out test sets; inference deterministic (no dropout).
special_cases: Models using multi-digit tokens (vocabulary-level multi-digit shortcuts) invalidate the per-digit token assumption; DAL then does not apply.
Very large models (>>10B parameters) may implement hybrid schemes that mix both mechanisms; DAL's strict L >= 6*D threshold applies to small-to-medium models (<=6B params).
If positional encoding is non-monotonic or learned in a way that bundles digits, DAL's layer-to-digit mapping may shift and require adapted thresholds.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Algorithmic tasks with clear sequential dependencies (e.g., carry propagation) can be implemented either by time/depth-serial computation or by parallel unrolled mappings; depth provides capacity for sequentialization.

inference: Digit-wise carry is a sequential, low-entropy operation across positions; reproducing it across depth requires roughly O(D) sequential steps; empirically a factor of ~6 layers per digit fits observed layer-utilization patterns in some small transformers trained on algorithmic tasks (inference rather than citation).

DAL predicts non-adjacent attention mass <=0.15 and sequential digit-local ablation patterns for models with L >= 6*D. If observed, DAL supported.
law.falsifies_if: Observed non-adjacent attention mass >0.30 for models with L >= 6*D, or layer-ablation produces immediate uniform failure across digits (no sequenced localized errors) for those models.

evaluation.explanation: The theory's central numeric threshold (L >= 6*D) appears to be an empirical claim without established basis in the literature. While transformers can implement both sequential and parallel computation strategies, the specific factor of 6 layers per digit lacks theoretical or empirical grounding in known results. The attention mass threshold of 0.15 is similarly arbitrary. The general principle that depth enables sequential computation is sound, but the precise quantitative claims are unsupported and likely oversimplified given the complexity of learned representations in transformers.

The theory provides highly concrete, measurable predictions: specific depth ratios (L >= 6*D), precise attention mass thresholds (<=0.15 for non-adjacent positions, >0.30 as falsifier), and detailed ablation patterns. Variables are operationalized and the experimental protocol is reproducible.

The theory synthesizes known concepts (depth-enabled sequentialization, algorithmic vs. memorization dichotomy in neural networks) into a specific quantitative framework for arithmetic tasks. The novelty lies in proposing a concrete numeric threshold and linking it to measurable attention/ablation signatures. While the general idea that depth matters for sequential tasks is well-established, the specific 6*D threshold and the operationalization via attention localization patterns represents a moderately novel empirical hypothesis rather than a fundamentally new principle.

The theory is highly testable with clear falsification criteria. The discriminating experiment is feasible, well-defined, and provides multiple independent test paths: attention mass measurement (with threshold 0.15 vs 0.30), layer ablation patterns, and depth variation experiments. The predictions are crisp and quantitative, making it straightforward to determine empirically whether the theory holds. law.theory_description: Addition is implemented by repeated localized attention 'sweeps' across digit positions that sequentially propagate carries. Each sweep resolves carries across a contiguous window of W digit positions; the model needs S nonredundant attention-update sweeps to propagate carries across D digits, and correct algorithmic addition occurs only if S >= ceil(D / W). When S < ceil(D / W) the model cannot reliably propagate long carry chains and instead relies on memorized heuristics or statistical shortcuts.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For exact addition of two D-digit base-10 integers tokenized at single digits, if the model provides S attention-update sweeps and attention locality window W (digits) such that S >= ceil(D / W) then carry propagation proceeds algorithmically by localized sweeps (Mechanism A dominates); if S < ceil(D / W) then carry propagation fails systematically and the model relies on memorized or statistical heuristics (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: S = ceil(D / W). Negative prediction: If S >= ceil(D / W), accuracy on inputs requiring maximal-length carry chains (e.g., 999... + 1) will NOT depend on the frequency of those examples in training and will remain high (>90%) even when such examples occur at frequency <= 1e-5 of training samples. Discriminating experiment: Train decoder-only transformers (vocabulary = digits) on D = 12 digit addition with three enforced attention locality masks producing effective W in {1,2,4}; for each W train model depths L in {8,12,24} and operationalize S as the number of sequential layers producing >5% positional-update norm (measured by per-position embedding change). Make maximal carry examples appear at frequency 1 / 100000 in the training stream. Measure exact-match accuracy on held-out maximal-carry cases and on random holds. If runs with measured S >= ceil(D/W) achieve >90% exact-match on maximal-carry cases despite their rarity and runs with S < ceil(D/W) fail (<40% accuracy) the CSAM statement is supported. Falsifier: Any configuration with measured S >= ceil(D / W) that, after extensive training, fails to reach >90% exact-match on rare maximal-carry cases (or whose performance on those cases scales linearly with example frequency rather than with S) refutes this statement.
law.domain_scope: Decoder-only transformers trained for token-level (single-digit) exact integer addition in base-10, where attention locality can be enforced by sparse-masking; S is measured as the count of layers producing nontrivial cross-position update (operationalized via embedding-change norm). Applies to models without architectural recurrent modules and without multi-digit tokenization.
special_cases: If tokenization groups multiple digits per token (k>1) the effective D and W should be scaled by k; CSAM then applies to token-level positions instead of digit-level.
If a model uses global attention every layer (W = D), then S >= 1 suffices and CSAM reduces to a simple depth threshold.
If residual paths are surgically altered (e.g., removed or scaled), the measured S may change and the ceil(D/W) bound must be recomputed using the new update norm threshold.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Hardware ripple-carry adders propagate carries sequentially across adjacent bits/digits; an analogous algorithmic requirement exists for any architecture constrained to local communication per update step.

inference: Attention implements the local communication channel in transformers; restricting attention locality W increases the number of sequential update steps required to move information across D positions, yielding the bound S >= ceil(D/W).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Analogy between ripple-carry adders and sequential neural propagation, and prior work connecting sparse/local attention to algorithmic computation.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: An explicit, falsifiable quantitative sweep-count bound S >= ceil(D/W), a concrete negative prediction about robustness to rare maximal-carry examples, and a precise discriminating experiment manipulating W and S.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Translates hardware adder intuition into an explicit transformer-attention bound that is directly testable and numerically specified.

law.discriminating_experiment: Train transformer decoders (embedding dim 512) on 12-digit addition with W enforced via sparse masks {1,2,4}. For each mask, train depths L in {8,12,24}. Define a layer as a sweep-step if mean L2 change of position embeddings after that layer >5% of initial embedding norm. Compute S as count of such layers. Insert maximal-carry examples at frequency 1/100k. Evaluate exact-match on maximal-carry held-out set and on random sums. Compare accuracy vs S and vs example frequency. Success pattern: accuracy >90% when S >= ceil(12/W) and <40% otherwise. Alternative patterns falsify CSAM.
law.falsifies_if: Measured S >= ceil(D/W) but model achieves <90% accuracy on rare maximal-carry cases after extensive training, or performance correlates strongly with example frequency and not S.

evaluation.explanation: The foundational claims (ripple-carry analogy, sequential propagation in local attention) align with established principles. However, the specific quantitative threshold S ≥ ceil(D/W) and the sharp >90% accuracy prediction are untested empirical claims rather than established facts. The theory avoids obvious factual errors but makes strong predictions that extend beyond current empirical knowledge.

The theory provides concrete operationalizations: specific values for W {1,2,4} and L {8,12,24}, a measurable definition of S (layers with >5% embedding-change norm), quantitative thresholds (ceil(D/W)), specific accuracy cutoffs (>90% vs <40%), and exact training conditions (maximal-carry frequency 1/100k). The 5% norm threshold is somewhat arbitrary but otherwise highly specific.

While the general intuition that local attention requires sequential propagation builds on known principles (ripple-carry adders, attention locality), the specific quantitative formulation S ≥ ceil(D/W) and especially the negative prediction about rare-example generalization (>90% accuracy at 1e-5 frequency) represent substantive novel contributions. This goes beyond mere restatement of existing ideas to provide a testable, formalized hypothesis.

The theory is highly falsifiable with clear experimental specifications: enforced attention masks, specific model depths, operationalized measurement of S via embedding norms, controlled training data with rare examples, and sharp quantitative predictions (>90% vs <40% accuracy). Multiple independent tests are possible by varying W, L, and D. The discriminating experiment is concrete and feasible with current tools.

The core mechanism is plausible and aligns with information-theoretic constraints on local communication. However, real transformers have complicating factors: parallel attention heads can partially circumvent strict locality, MLPs between layers enable nonlinear mixing, and residual connections create multiple propagation paths. The sharp quantitative threshold and >90% accuracy claim may be optimistic given these complexities, though the directional prediction remains sound.

While related work exists on arithmetic in transformers and attention patterns, I am not aware of published experiments that directly test this theory's specific predictions: enforcing controlled attention locality W, measuring sweep count S via embedding-change norms, testing the ceil(D/W) threshold, or examining maximal-carry generalization at specified low frequencies. The theory appears largely untested in its specific formulation.
