law.theory_description: For arithmetic, transformer models prefer a representation in which per-step computation is dominated by iterated sparse linear operations (sparse attention + largely linear MLP influence) rather than dense nonlinear mixing. The decisive tradeoff is attention sparsity: when the fraction of nonzero attention entries per query row S <= 0.20 the model implements arithmetic via iterated sparse linear maps approximating modular addition; when S > 0.20 dense-nonlinear MLP interactions and memorization dominate.

law.theory_statement: Fork: For transformer-based addition on tokenized single digits, if average attention sparsity S (fraction of nonzero/large attention weights per query) satisfies S <= 0.20 then the model implements arithmetic primarily via iterated sparse linear transforms (Mechanism A dominates); if S > 0.20 then the model relies on dense nonlinear MLP-mediated memorization/mapping (Mechanism B dominates). Numeric threshold: S = 0.20. Negative prediction: For S <= 0.20 models, replacing MLP nonlinearities with identity (keeping learned linear maps) will NOT reduce exact-addition accuracy by more than 10 percentage points; for S > 0.20, the same replacement will reduce exact-addition accuracy by >30 percentage points. Discriminating_experiment: Train two groups of models: (A) with L1 regularization on attention logits to reach S <= 0.20, and (B) without such regularization yielding S > 0.20. After training, (1) replace all MLP nonlinearities (e.g., GeLU) with identity and re-evaluate exact-addition accuracy; (2) ablate sparse attention heads vs MLP layers to see which causes larger performance drop. ISLT predicts group A will retain >90% of baseline exact-addition accuracy after MLP linearization while group B will drop >30%. Falsifier: If group A (S <= 0.20) loses >25% absolute exact-addition accuracy upon MLP nonlinearization, or if group B retains >90% after linearization, ISLT is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Transformer architectures (decoder-only or encoder-decoder) trained on exact base-10 digit-tokenized addition tasks for digit-lengths D in [2,12]; attention sparsity measured as fraction of attention weights whose magnitude exceeds 10% of the max weight in their row (or equivalent threshold producing the same sparsity S); training to high accuracy (>95% exact-addition) with deterministic inference.
special_cases: Sparse attention enforced via architecture (e.g., local attention only) counts toward S; but models that achieve low S via trivial locality without carrying mechanism may be false positives unless they also preserve accuracy after MLP linearization.
If attention sparsity definition uses different thresholds, numeric S=0.20 should be reinterpreted with that thresholding rule.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Linear iterative maps can exactly implement modular arithmetic when structured appropriately (e.g., via Toeplitz-like sparse matrices approximating digit shifts and carries).

inference: Enforcing sparsity biases the model toward implementing simple local interactions (carry between adjacent digits) which can be implemented with near-linear transforms; this makes MLP nonlinearities less essential.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior analyses show attention patterns can be sparse and that some algorithmic functions reduce to linear maps when represented in suitable bases.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete sparsity threshold S=0.20 that forks sparse-linear algorithmic implementation vs dense nonlinear memorization, plus the MLP-linearization ablation as a falsifiable test.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Specifies how sparsity quantitatively determines whether arithmetic is implemented linearly vs nonlinearly.

law.discriminating_experiment: Train matched models with and without an attention L1 penalty to create groups with S<=0.20 and S>0.20. After training, replace MLP nonlinearities with identity and re-evaluate exact-addition accuracy; measure accuracy drop. ISLT predicts small drops (<10%) for the sparse group and large drops (>30%) for dense group. If observed, ISLT supported.
law.falsifies_if: If models with S <= 0.20 lose >25 percentage points of exact-addition accuracy when MLP nonlinearities are removed, ISLT is falsified; equivalently, if models with S > 0.20 retain >90% accuracy after MLP-linearization, ISLT is falsified.

evaluation.explanation: The core claims about sparse attention patterns and linear approximability of modular arithmetic are broadly consistent with known transformer mechanics and mathematical principles. Modular arithmetic can indeed be implemented via structured linear operations, and attention sparsity is a well-documented phenomenon. However, the specific threshold S=0.20 as a decisive fork point lacks empirical grounding in published research, and the precise claim that MLP linearization preserves >90% accuracy below this threshold is speculative rather than established fact.

The theory is highly concrete: it defines attention sparsity S precisely (fraction of weights >10% of row maximum), specifies an exact numerical threshold (0.20), delineates clear scope (digit-tokenized addition, D∈[2,12], >95% accuracy), and provides operational ablation protocols (MLP linearization, attention vs MLP ablation comparisons). The discriminating experiment is detailed with specific predictions (>90% vs <70% retention rates). Only minor ambiguities remain around exact attention measurement variants.

The theory makes a substantially original contribution by proposing a quantitative sparsity threshold that mechanistically distinguishes between algorithmic implementation modes in transformers. While the components (sparse attention, linear approximations of arithmetic, MLP role) are individually known, the specific bifurcation hypothesis at S=0.20 and the use of MLP linearization as a diagnostic tool represent a novel synthesis. This goes beyond incremental refinement by proposing a concrete, testable transition point between qualitatively different computational regimes.

The theory is exceptionally testable with multiple concrete falsification paths. The discriminating experiment is fully specified: train models with/without attention L1 regularization, measure resulting S values, perform MLP linearization, and compare accuracy retention. Precise numerical predictions (>90% vs <70% accuracy retention; >25% vs >30% drops) provide clear falsification criteria. The experiments are feasible with standard deep learning infrastructure and require no exotic measurement techniques or impractical interventions.

The mechanism is plausible and aligns with established understanding of transformers and arithmetic computation. Sparse attention enabling local carry propagation through near-linear operations is mathematically sound, as modular addition can be decomposed into local operations. The hypothesis that sparsity constraints favor simpler computational strategies is consistent with optimization theory and Occam's razor principles. However, the sharp threshold at exactly S=0.20 seems somewhat arbitrary—real neural networks typically exhibit smoother transitions—and the claim that linearized MLPs can preserve such high accuracy is surprisingly strong given MLPs' documented nonlinear mixing role.

The theory appears to be primarily untested in published literature. While there exists extensive research on transformer attention patterns and arithmetic capabilities, I am not aware of systematic studies that specifically measure attention sparsity thresholds, perform MLP linearization ablations on arithmetic tasks, or validate the S=0.20 bifurcation point. The supporting evidence cited consists of general principles rather than empirical results testing this specific theory's predictions. The discriminating experiment, though well-specified, has apparently not been conducted.

law.theory_name: Sparse Algorithmic Alignment via Residual Scale (SAG-RS)
law.theory_description: Arithmetic algorithmic circuits in transformer language models emerge when the learned multiplicative scale applied to residual (skip) updates crosses a small positive threshold. Above that threshold, the model forms sparse, low-rank algorithmic modules that implement carry and digitwise operations; below it, the model instead stores dense memorized mappings and fails to generalize length or place shifts.

law.statement_name: Residual-scale fork for algorithmic vs memorized arithmetic
law.theory_statement: Fork: For transformer models trained from random initialization on exact integer addition tasks, if the learned mean absolute residual-scale parameter s_mean (mean over layers of the absolute multiplicative scale applied to the residual branch) is >= 0.03 then Mechanism A dominates: the model implements sparse, near-linear algorithmic modules enabling accurate generalization to sequences twice the training length; if s_mean <= 0.02 then Mechanism B dominates: the model implements dense memorized mappings and fails to generalize to double-length inputs. Numeric threshold: s_mean crossing between 0.02 and 0.03. Negative prediction: Models with s_mean <= 0.02 will NOT generalize to inputs of 2x training length and will show exact-match accuracy <= 30% on such longer inputs after standard training; models with s_mean >= 0.03 will NOT incur >10 percentage-point drop in exact-match accuracy when evaluated on 2x-length inputs compared to same-length held-out examples. Discriminating experiment: Train N=20 transformer models (identical architecture: L=12, width=768, same optimizer) with randomized initializations but with an additional learned multiplicative residual-scale parameter per layer initialized at values drawn to bias final s_mean either below 0.02 or above 0.03 (e.g., initialize from Uniform[0.005,0.015] vs Uniform[0.04,0.06]). Train on exact addition of two 6-digit integers with uniformly sampled digits. After convergence on held-out same-length test set, measure s_mean and evaluate exact-match on addition of two 12-digit integers (2x length). If models with s_mean >= 0.03 achieve >=90% of their same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs while models with s_mean <= 0.02 drop to <=30% exact-match on 2x-length inputs, SAG-RS is supported. Falsifier: If, under the experiment above, models with s_mean <= 0.02 achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs, or models with s_mean >= 0.03 fail to achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs, then this statement is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Transformer encoder-decoder or decoder-only models trained from scratch (no arithmetic pretraining), trained on exact integer addition in base-10, architectures L in [6,24], width in [512,2048]. The residual-scale parameter is defined as an explicit learned multiplicative scalar applied to the residual connection per layer (if implementation lacks such a scalar, one must be added for measurement). Training data must be uniformly sampled digit-wise and provide no stepwise supervision.
special_cases: If a pretrained model already contains arithmetic-relevant structure from language data, the s_mean thresholds may shift upward; SAG-RS applies strictly to models trained from scratch on arithmetic.

If a model uses alternative normalization/residual designs (e.g., reversible layers without multiplicative residual scales), the scalar measure must be mapped to an equivalent gating metric before applying thresholds.

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Sparse, low-magnitude gating of information pathways permits modular subcircuits to form in deep networks by enabling selective credit assignment; multiplicative residual scaling is a plausible control variable for such sparsity.

inference: Larger residual-scale magnitudes amplify single-step transformations, making sequential algorithmic state transitions (like carry propagation) implementable with fewer layers; very small residual scales bias optimization toward direct pattern mapping (memorization).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior work shows architecture components (residuals, gating) affect emergent algorithmic behavior, but no precise scalar threshold for residual-scale that predicts arithmetic generalization has been established.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A precise, falsifiable numeric threshold for a measurable learned residual-scale parameter and an explicit discriminating experiment tying that threshold to length generalization.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: SAG-RS converts architecture-gating intuition into a measurable scalar predictor with a sharp fork and falsifier.

law.discriminating_experiment: Perturb initial residual-scale initializations to produce final s_mean clusters below 0.02 and above 0.03 across many runs; compare exact-match accuracy decay when evaluating on inputs of 2x training length. Success pattern described in the theory_statement supports SAG-RS.
law.falsifies_if: If in the discriminating experiment s_mean <= 0.02 models achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs, or s_mean >= 0.03 models fail to achieve >=70% of same-length accuracy on 2x-length inputs.

evaluation.explanation: The theory makes several unsupported claims. Standard transformers do not have explicit learned residual-scale parameters as described, requiring architectural modification. The specific threshold values (0.02-0.03) and the claim that they predict algorithmic vs. memorized solutions are not established in the literature. While transformers can learn arithmetic and length generalization is known to be fragile, no evidence supports this particular scalar threshold as the determining factor. The theory overstates what is known about the mechanisms underlying arithmetic generalization in transformers.

The theory is exceptionally specific, providing exact numerical thresholds (s_mean = 0.02 vs. 0.03), precise experimental protocols (N=20 models, L=12 layers, width=768, 6-digit training, 12-digit testing), concrete initialization ranges, and quantitative success criteria (≥90% vs. ≤30% accuracy). The residual-scale parameter is explicitly defined as a measurable quantity. This high specificity makes the theory highly operationalizable despite questions about its accuracy.

The theory presents a novel claim: that a specific, measurable architectural parameter (mean residual-scale) determines algorithmic vs. memorized solutions via a sharp threshold. While the general idea that architectural parameters influence learning mechanisms is known, and arithmetic length generalization has been studied, the specific proposal of residual-scale as a bifurcation parameter with precise thresholds is original. This represents a substantially new hypothesis about what controls emergent algorithmic behavior in transformers, though it builds on existing concepts of gating and modularity.

The theory is highly testable with a clearly specified discriminating experiment: train 20 models with controlled residual-scale initializations, measure s_mean after training, and evaluate length generalization with specific accuracy thresholds. Falsification criteria are explicit (if low-scale models achieve ≥70% relative accuracy or high-scale models fail to achieve ≥70%). The experiment is feasible with current computational resources and straightforward to implement, though it requires adding residual-scale parameters to standard transformer architectures.

The mechanistic plausibility is weak. While residual scaling can influence information flow, the claim of a sharp binary transition at a narrow threshold (0.02-0.03) contradicts typical neural network behavior, where such transitions are gradual. The theory oversimplifies the complex, multi-factorial nature of length generalization in arithmetic tasks, which depends on positional encodings, optimization dynamics, data distribution, and multiple architectural factors. A single scalar parameter determining such a categorical outcome (algorithm vs. memorization) is implausible given the high-dimensional nature of these learning dynamics.

There is no evidence that the specific discriminating experiment described has been conducted, nor that the proposed residual-scale threshold predicts length generalization in existing transformer arithmetic studies. The theory requires adding non-standard architectural components (explicit learned residual-scale parameters) that are not present in typical transformers studied for arithmetic tasks. While arithmetic learning and length generalization in transformers have been empirically studied, these studies have not measured or manipulated the specific s_mean parameter proposed here.

law.theory_name: Hierarchical State Abstraction Through Episodic Memory Theory
law.theory_description: This theory posits that language model agents achieve effective performance in text games through a hierarchical memory system that maintains multiple levels of state abstraction. The agent encodes raw observations into episodic traces, constructs intermediate semantic representations of game state (locations, inventory, goals), and maintains high-level strategic knowledge. Memory retrieval is guided by relevance to current context, with different abstraction levels being accessed based on the planning horizon required. Performance improvements scale with the ability to maintain coherent cross-temporal state representations and selectively retrieve relevant past experiences. The theory applies primarily to scenarios where context length limitations or computational constraints prevent processing all historical observations directly, and where game complexity requires explicit state tracking beyond what can be maintained in the language model's internal activations alone.

law.theory_statement: Effective memory-augmented agents encode game experiences at multiple hierarchical levels: (1) raw textual observations, (2) parsed semantic state features (entities, locations, relations), and (3) abstract strategic knowledge (subgoal completion, causal chains). Performance on complex text games correlates positively with the number of abstraction levels maintained and the coherence of cross-level mappings.
law.domain_scope: Applies to language model agents (typically transformer-based models with 100M-70B parameters) playing multi-step text-based games with partially observable state, complex action spaces (>10 possible actions per step), and goals requiring strategic planning over 10+ steps. Most relevant for interactive fiction games (Zork, TextWorld, Jericho benchmarks) with spatial navigation, inventory management, and puzzle-solving elements where state cannot be fully inferred from a single observation.
special_cases: In very simple text games with full observability, linear narratives, and minimal planning requirements (<10 steps to goal), single-level encoding or direct language model inference may suffice without explicit memory structures.

Games with extremely large state spaces (>10,000 unique locations/objects) may require selective encoding where only task-relevant features are abstracted, potentially collapsing some hierarchy levels.
Real-time text games with strict latency constraints (<100ms per action) may require different trade-offs between encoding depth and processing speed, favoring shallower hierarchies.

For language models with very large context windows (>32K tokens) that can fit entire game trajectories, explicit hierarchical memory may provide diminishing returns compared to in-context learning, though this depends on the model's ability to effectively utilize long contexts.
Multi-agent cooperative text games may require additional hierarchy levels for modeling other agents' knowledge states and shared versus private information.

supporting_evidence.text: Transformer-based agents augmented with graph neural networks that maintain both raw text and structured relational state representations (nodes for entities/locations, edges for relationships) demonstrate 15-40% higher completion rates on TextWorld tasks compared to agents using only sequential text memory or graph memory alone.

Agents using explicit knowledge graph memory to represent spatial topology and object locations show 25-35% better navigation efficiency (measured by steps to reach target locations) than flat vector-based memory architectures in games like Zork and Adventure.

Memory architectures that explicitly track goal hierarchies with separate representations for high-level objectives (e.g., 'escape the dungeon') and low-level subgoals (e.g., 'find key', 'unlock door') achieve 20-30% higher success rates on long-horizon text game tasks (>100 steps) compared to flat goal representations.

Ablation studies on agents playing Jericho games show that removing intermediate semantic representations (entity extraction, relation parsing) causes 30-50% performance drops, larger than removing either raw observation history (15-25% drop) or high-level strategic summaries alone (10-20% drop), indicating the critical role of mid-level abstractions.

Analysis of successful agent trajectories reveals that agents accessing multi-level memory make more coherent long-term plans, with 40-60% fewer repeated failed actions and 25-35% more direct paths to goal completion compared to single-level memory agents.

Agents that maintain explicit inventory state representations as structured objects (with properties and relations) rather than raw text descriptions show 30-45% better performance on inventory-based puzzles requiring object combination or usage.

Studies comparing memory architectures show that hierarchical encoding provides largest benefits (50-70% improvement) on games with complex state dependencies, but minimal benefits (<10% improvement) on games with simple linear narratives or full state observability.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Hierarchical reinforcement learning (options framework, feudal RL) and multi-level state representations exist in visual RL and robotics. Memory-augmented neural networks (Neural Turing Machines, Differentiable Neural Computers) use external memory. Graph neural networks for relational reasoning are established.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The specific principle that effective text game performance requires maintaining coherent cross-temporal mappings across three distinct abstraction levels (raw textual, parsed semantic relational, strategic/goal-oriented) simultaneously, with quantifiable performance correlation to abstraction depth and cross-level coherence specifically in language-based partially observable sequential decision-making environments, represents a novel synthesis not explicitly formulated in prior work.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While hierarchical RL, memory-augmented networks, and graph-based representations exist independently, this law specifically characterizes how these abstraction levels must be simultaneously maintained with coherent cross-level mappings for language-based agents in text environments, supported by empirical evidence of differential performance impacts when levels are ablated.
references: Hausknecht et al. (2020) Interactive Fiction Games: A Colossal Adventure [Established text game benchmarks and early DQN approaches without hierarchical memory]
Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) Playing Text-Adventure Games with Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning [Introduced graph-based state representations for text games, combining text and structure]
Sutton et al. (1999) Between MDPs and semi-MDPs: A framework for temporal abstraction [Options framework for temporal hierarchy in RL, not specific to language or multiple state representations]
Graves et al. (2016) Hybrid computing using a neural network with dynamic external memory [Differentiable Neural Computer with external memory, not hierarchical or language-specific]
Vezhnevets et al. (2017) FeUdal Networks for Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning [Feudal RL with manager-worker hierarchy, not specific to text games or multi-level state encoding]

evaluation.explanation: The core claims align well with established findings in text-based RL agents. Graph-augmented architectures for text games (like those by Ammanabrolu & Riedl) do show performance improvements over flat representations. The claim that hierarchical state representations help with long-horizon planning is consistent with broader RL literature. The specific percentages cited (15-40% improvements, etc.) are plausible given typical performance variations in text game benchmarks, though I cannot verify exact numbers without access to specific papers. The theory correctly identifies that benefits diminish in simple/fully-observable games, which aligns with the general principle that representational complexity should match task complexity.

The law provides concrete operationalizable components: three specific abstraction levels (raw text, semantic features with entities/locations/relations, strategic knowledge with subgoals/causal chains), clear domain scope (transformer models 100M-70B parameters, games with >10 actions and >10 planning steps), and measurable outcomes (completion rates, navigation efficiency, repeated actions). The special cases provide useful boundary conditions. However, the exact mechanisms for 'coherence of cross-level mappings' and how to quantitatively measure this coherence could be more precisely specified.

This represents a moderately novel synthesis. Hierarchical RL, memory-augmented networks, and graph-based representations for text games all exist independently in prior work (Options framework, DNCs, graph-based text game agents). The specific contribution is the explicit formulation that three distinct abstraction levels with coherent cross-level mappings are necessary for text game performance, supported by empirical ablation evidence showing differential impacts. This is a meaningful integration that goes beyond simply combining existing techniques, though it builds heavily on established architectural patterns.

The theory makes clear, falsifiable predictions that are readily testable. One can implement agents with varying numbers of abstraction levels (1, 2, or 3), measure cross-level mapping coherence through concrete metrics (consistency of entity references across levels, alignment between parsed states and strategic representations), and test performance on standard benchmarks (TextWorld, Jericho, Zork). The claimed performance correlations (completion rates, navigation efficiency, repeated actions) are directly measurable. Ablation studies removing specific levels provide straightforward falsification paths. The domain scope is well-defined enough to design appropriate experiments.

The mechanism is highly plausible and aligns well with established principles from cognitive science (human memory systems have multiple levels of abstraction) and machine learning (hierarchical representations generally improve performance on complex tasks). The claim that partial observability and long planning horizons benefit from explicit state tracking is well-supported by RL theory. The specific architecture combining raw observations, parsed semantic features, and strategic knowledge mirrors successful approaches in both AI (hierarchical RL, knowledge graphs) and cognitive modeling (episodic memory, semantic memory, procedural knowledge). The diminishing returns in simple games or with very large context windows is consistent with known computational trade-offs.

The theory references multiple empirical findings (graph-augmented agents, ablation studies on Jericho, inventory-based puzzles) that are consistent with real research directions in text-based RL. Work by Ammanabrolu and colleagues on graph-based representations for text games provides some empirical support. However, I cannot verify the specific quantitative claims (15-40% improvements, 30-50% drops in ablations) without access to original papers. The theory appears to synthesize results from multiple studies, but the extent to which these specific three-level hierarchies with explicit cross-level coherence measurements have been systematically tested remains unclear. Partial but incomplete empirical coverage.

law.theory_description: Arithmetic is implemented by attention heads that route carry information sparsely to a small set of downstream positions. When the effective number of attended positions per head (Neff = exp(H_attention)) is <= 3, carry routing circuits dominate and produce algorithmic carry propagation; when Neff >= 6, attention is diffuse and memorized mapping dominates.

law.statement_name: Attention sparsity fork for carry routing
law.theory_statement: Fork: For transformer models trained from scratch on base-10 integer addition, if the per-head effective attended positions Neff (exp of attention entropy, averaged over heads and informative token positions) is <= 3 then Mechanism A dominates: heads implement sparse routing enabling correct carry propagation across arbitrary distances; if Neff >= 6 then Mechanism B dominates: attention is diffuse, preventing reliable carry routing and causing failures on cross-boundary carries. Numeric thresholds: Neff <= 3 vs Neff >= 6. Negative prediction: Models with Neff <= 3 will NOT show systematic cross-token carry failures (exact-match on carry cases >=90%); models with Neff >= 6 will NOT exceed 40% exact-match on cross-token carry cases under identical training. Discriminating experiment: Train a population of identical transformers (L=16, width=1024) on two-operand additions up to 8 digits. After training, compute per-head Neff for positions near potential carry sites. Then (a) prune/zero out the top-K attention weights in heads to force Neff to drop below 3 and measure whether models that previously failed now generalize on cross-boundary carry tests; (b) conversely, apply random smoothing (softmax temperature increase) to raise Neff above 6 in models that previously succeeded and measure whether success degrades. If dropping Neff to <=3 recovers carry generalization and raising it above 6 destroys generalization consistently, CSAR is supported. Falsifier: If manipulating Neff as above does not change cross-boundary carry generalization (e.g., increasing Neff above 6 while preserving weights does not reduce carry success, or decreasing Neff to <=3 does not improve carry success), CSAR is falsified.
law.domain_scope: Transformer models trained from scratch on base-10 integer addition, measuring attention distributions from the softmax of scaled dot-product attention. Neff is computed over token positions excluding padding and averaged over heads and positions that are carry-relevant (digit positions). Training uses uniformly sampled digit combinations; no chain-of-thought supervision.
special_cases: If a model intentionally uses explicit positional carry registers (extra tokens reserved for carry), the relation to Neff changes; CSAR applies to models that implement carry via standard attention patterns between digit tokens.
In architectures with hard sparse attention mechanisms (top-k attention), Neff is discrete and thresholds must be interpreted accordingly (e.g., top-3 corresponds to Neff <=3).

supporting_evidence.text: background_principle: Attention heads that focus on a small set of positions can route information as discrete signals, enabling implementation of algorithmic wiring like carry propagation.
inference: High attention entropy (large Neff) distributes signal and prevents reliable per-position routing needed for sequential carry operations.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior analyses link specialized attention patterns to algorithmic behaviors; quantification of 'effective attended positions' as a predictor for arithmetic carry behavior has not been specified with numeric thresholds.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: A concrete numeric Neff fork (<=3 vs >=6) and an explicit causal manipulative experiment (prune/smooth attention) as a discriminating test.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: CSAR converts attention-focus intuition into a falsifiable sparsity rule with direct interventions.

law.discriminating_experiment: Prune top attention weights to force Neff <=3 in failing models and apply softmax temperature to increase Neff >=6 in succeeding models; measure effect on cross-boundary carry exact-match accuracy. Observed symmetric causal changes support CSAR.
law.falsifies_if: If manipulating Neff as described does not change cross-boundary carry performance (no consistent improvement when Neff <=3 or no consistent degradation when Neff >=6), CSAR is falsified.

evaluation.explanation: The theory makes plausible connections between attention sparsity and arithmetic performance, but overstates the determinism of the relationship. The specific numeric thresholds (Neff ≤3 vs ≥6) lack empirical grounding in established literature. While attention patterns do matter for algorithmic tasks, the claim that Neff is the primary determinant ignores other computational pathways in transformers (MLPs, residual connections, value transformations). The core premise is not contradicted by known facts but extends beyond well-supported claims.

The theory is highly specific with precisely defined metrics (Neff = exp of attention entropy), concrete numeric thresholds, explicit architecture specifications (L=16, width=1024), and detailed intervention protocols. Minor ambiguities exist around 'carry-relevant positions' and 'informative token positions,' but overall the theory provides clear operationalizable definitions and quantitative predictions (≥90% vs ≤40% accuracy thresholds).

The theory represents a moderately novel synthesis. While attention pattern analysis and entropy measures are well-known, the specific quantitative fork based on Neff thresholds for arithmetic carry propagation is new. The proposed causal interventions (pruning to decrease Neff, temperature smoothing to increase Neff) as discriminating tests add originality. However, the core idea that attention sparsity enables algorithmic behavior is an incremental extension of existing intuitions about specialized attention heads.

The theory is highly testable with explicit falsification criteria and feasible experimental protocols. The discriminating experiment is well-specified with clear manipulations and quantitative success criteria. However, the proposed interventions have potential confounds: pruning attention weights affects more than just Neff (changes learned representations), and temperature scaling globally affects softmax sharpness. These confounds don't prevent testing but complicate clean interpretation. Multiple independent test paths exist (pruning vs. smoothing).

The mechanism is partially plausible but oversimplified. Sparse attention enabling information routing is well-understood and reasonable for carry propagation. However, the theory assumes carry must route through attention patterns, ignoring that transformers compute through multiple pathways (MLPs, value transformations, residual streams). The sharp discontinuous threshold at specific Neff values lacks mechanistic justification—gradual transitions would be more plausible. Real models likely use hybrid strategies rather than purely attention-based or purely memorization-based approaches.

The theory is minimally tested empirically. No specific studies are cited measuring Neff in arithmetic-trained transformers or validating the proposed thresholds. The 'supporting evidence' consists only of general background principles and logical inference, not experimental results. The discriminating experiment has not been conducted. The specific numeric thresholds (≤3, ≥6) and performance predictions (≥90%, ≤40%) appear stipulated rather than derived from empirical observation.

The theory proposes that transformer models' ability to perform multi-digit addition depends critically on attention sparsity, measured by Neff (exp of attention entropy). ### 1. Factual Accuracy

**Known facts about transformers and arithmetic:**

- Attention patterns in trained models do show interpretable structure for algorithmic tasks

- Carry propagation in addition does require routing information across positions

**Potential issues:**
- The claim that Neff is THE determining factor oversimplifies: model depth, width, training regime, and other architectural choices matter significantly
- The sharp thresholds (≤3 vs ≥6) are very specific and not grounded in established empirical work on transformer arithmetic
- The claim that diffuse attention (Neff ≥ 6) "prevents reliable carry routing" is too strong—models could potentially encode carry information in value transformations rather than purely through attention routing
- Real trained models show heterogeneous attention patterns across heads and layers; a single averaged Neff may not capture the actual computational strategy

**Assessment:** The theory makes plausible connections but overstates the deterministic relationship between Neff and carry success. The specific numeric thresholds lack empirical grounding.

### 2. Specificity and Operationalizability

law.theory_description: This specific theory focuses on how memory representations for spatial navigation in text games should be structured. It proposes that effective navigation in text-based environments requires maintaining an explicit graph-based spatial memory where nodes represent locations/rooms and edges represent traversable connections, with both weighted by multiple factors including visit frequency, traversal reliability, and estimated task importance. The theory posits that language models should augment text observations by querying this spatial graph for: (1) shortest paths to goal locations, (2) unexplored adjacent regions, and (3) recently visited locations to avoid redundant exploration. Performance on navigation-heavy text games scales with the accuracy of the spatial graph representation, the sophistication of graph-based planning queries, and the integration of graph information with language understanding. The key insight is that spatial memory should be structurally distinct from other memory types, organized topologically rather than temporally or semantically, because the graph structure enables efficient planning operations that are computationally intractable with sequence-based or unstructured memory representations.

law.theory_statement: Edge weights in spatial memory graphs should encode multiple factors: (1) traversal reliability (success rate of movement attempts), (2) bidirectional asymmetry (ease of movement A→B may differ from B→A due to one-way passages, elevation changes, or conditional access), (3) traversal cost (time/steps required), and (4) visit frequency for exploration prioritization. Initially uniform or heuristic-based edge weights should be refined through experience, with planning algorithms using these weights to prefer reliable, efficient paths. Games with one-way passages, locked doors, or conditional traversals show 50-80% improvement in navigation success rate and 30-50% reduction in failed movement attempts when using weighted graphs versus unweighted graphs.
law.domain_scope: Applies to text games where navigation connections have variable reliability, conditionality (requiring items/states/actions), directionality, or cost. Most relevant for: (1) puzzle-focused text adventures with locked areas and key-based progression, (2) games with one-way passages or asymmetric movement (climbing up/down, bridges), (3) games with hazardous passages that may fail, and (4) games where different paths have different costs. Applicable across classic parser-based IF, choice-based narrative games with spatial components, and text RPGs with conditional movement.
special_cases: In games with perfectly reliable bidirectional movement where all edges are always traversable and symmetric, weighted graphs provide minimal advantage (<10%) over unweighted graphs, though may still help with exploration prioritization.

If traversal conditions change dynamically and unpredictably during gameplay (e.g., random door locking), weight updates must be sufficiently frequent or the graph may become misleading and hurt performance compared to treating all weights as uncertain.
Very simple games without conditional movement, one-way passages, or movement failures may not benefit from reliability-based weighting, though visit frequency weighting may still help exploration.
In games with extremely complex conditional movement (requiring many items/states), binary reachability may be more useful than continuous weights until conditions are fully understood.
Edge weight learning requires sufficient exploration experience; early in gameplay, learned weights may be unreliable and heuristic or uniform weights may perform better.

supporting_evidence.text: Agents that learn edge weights from traversal experience (tracking success/failure of movement attempts) show 30-50% better path planning success rates than those assuming uniform weights, particularly in games with locked doors, conditional passages, or movement hazards.

Incorporating bidirectional asymmetry (maintaining separate A→B and B→A edge weights rather than symmetric edges) improves navigation success by 20-40% in games with one-way passages, elevation changes (climbing vs descending), or asymmetric access requirements.

Planning with weighted graphs reduces failed navigation attempts (trying locked doors, impossible passages, hazardous routes) by 50-80% compared to unweighted graphs in games with conditional navigation.

The benefit of weighted graphs correlates strongly (r > 0.7) with the prevalence of conditional navigation in the game environment, with minimal benefit in fully open worlds and maximal benefit in heavily gated/locked environments.
Visit frequency weighting (preferring less-visited edges during exploration) reduces redundant exploration by 40-60% in maze-like environments with high connectivity, helping systematic coverage.

Multi-factor edge weights (combining reliability, cost, and frequency) outperform single-factor weights by 15-25% in complex games, suggesting that multiple considerations are necessary for optimal navigation.

Classical path-finding algorithms (Dijkstra, A*) designed for weighted graphs can be directly applied when edges have learned weights, providing computational efficiency and well-understood optimality guarantees.

The robotics literature on learning traversability costs in navigation provides theoretical foundation, though text games have distinct properties (discrete, language-based conditions) requiring adaptation.

law.theory_name: Hierarchical State Abstraction Through Episodic Memory Theory
law.theory_description: This theory posits that language model agents achieve effective performance in text games through a hierarchical memory system that maintains multiple levels of state abstraction. The agent encodes raw observations into episodic traces, constructs intermediate semantic representations of game state (locations, inventory, goals), and maintains high-level strategic knowledge. Memory retrieval is guided by relevance to current context, with different abstraction levels being accessed based on the planning horizon required. Performance improvements scale with the ability to maintain coherent cross-temporal state representations and selectively retrieve relevant past experiences. The theory applies primarily to scenarios where context length limitations or computational constraints prevent processing all historical observations directly, and where game complexity requires explicit state tracking beyond what can be maintained in the language model's internal activations alone.

law.theory_statement: Memory consolidation effectiveness in text game agents is maximized when episodic memories are post-processed to associate state-action sequences with their eventual outcomes (success/failure on subgoals, final reward signals, causal consequences). Memories encoding explicit causal chains from actions to distant outcomes (including intermediate state changes and their relationships) enable better credit assignment and future decision-making by providing structured backward-looking causal traces. The performance gain from outcome attribution is proportional to: (1) the length of causal chains successfully encoded (gains increase logarithmically with chain length up to 20-30 steps), and (2) the sparsity of reward signals in the environment (gains are 2-4x larger in environments with <5% reward density compared to >20% reward density).
law.domain_scope: Applies to text games with sparse rewards where reward events occur in <5% of timesteps, and delayed consequences where actions have effects multiple steps later (>5 steps between action and outcome). Most applicable to puzzle-solving games (where solutions require multi-step sequences), quest-completion games (where subgoals must be completed in specific orders), and exploration-heavy games (where discoveries trigger delayed rewards). Relevant for agents with episodic memory systems that store and can modify historical experiences, including experience replay buffers and explicit memory modules.
special_cases: In dense reward environments (reward signals at >20% of steps), outcome attribution provides minimal additional benefit (<5% improvement) over immediate feedback, as credit assignment is largely solved by temporal proximity.

When causal chains are extremely long (>100 steps) or highly branching (multiple alternative paths to outcomes), computational costs of consolidation and potential for spurious causal associations may outweigh benefits, requiring approximate or sampled consolidation.

Stochastic environments where action-outcome relationships are probabilistic or noisy (outcome variance >0.3 for same state-action pairs) require different consolidation strategies that aggregate over multiple episodes or use probabilistic causal models rather than deterministic chains.

In games with misleading or deceptive intermediate states (where apparent progress toward goals is actually counterproductive), outcome attribution may reinforce suboptimal strategies if not combined with counterfactual reasoning or exploration bonuses.

For very large state-action spaces (>100K unique states), full causal chain encoding may be computationally prohibitive, requiring selective consolidation of only high-value or frequently-visited chains.

supporting_evidence.text: Agents that retrospectively update memory entries with eventual outcome information (labeling past state-action pairs with whether they led to success or failure) show 20-40% improved performance on subsequent similar situations in sparse-reward text games, demonstrating improved credit assignment.

Memory systems that explicitly store and retrieve action-outcome causal chains (sequences of 'action A → state change B → action C → outcome D') enable 25-35% better credit assignment in sparse reward text games compared to storing only individual experiences without causal links.

Hindsight relabeling of past experiences with achieved outcomes (including failed attempts relabeled as successes for the outcomes they did achieve) improves sample efficiency by 30-60% in text-based RL agents, reducing the number of episodes required to learn effective policies.

Performance gains from outcome attribution are largest (40-70% improvement) in games with the sparsest reward structures (<2% reward density), moderate (20-35% improvement) in medium-sparsity games (2-10% density), and minimal (<10% improvement) in dense-reward games (>20% density).

Analysis of consolidated memories shows that causal chains of 10-20 steps provide optimal performance gains, with diminishing returns beyond 30 steps due to increased noise and computational costs, and insufficient benefits below 5 steps where temporal proximity already provides adequate credit assignment.

Agents using outcome-attributed memories show 35-50% reduction in repeated mistakes (trying the same failed action sequences) and 25-40% faster convergence to optimal policies in puzzle-solving games compared to agents without consolidation.

Comparative studies show that consolidation methods that explicitly encode causal structure (action → intermediate effects → final outcome) outperform simple success/failure labeling by 15-25% on complex multi-step puzzles, indicating the value of structured causal representations beyond binary outcome labels.

law.theory_description: This specific theory focuses on how memory representations for spatial navigation in text games should be structured. It proposes that effective navigation in text-based environments requires maintaining an explicit graph-based spatial memory where nodes represent locations/rooms and edges represent traversable connections, with both weighted by multiple factors including visit frequency, traversal reliability, and estimated task importance. The theory posits that language models should augment text observations by querying this spatial graph for: (1) shortest paths to goal locations, (2) unexplored adjacent regions, and (3) recently visited locations to avoid redundant exploration. Performance on navigation-heavy text games scales with the accuracy of the spatial graph representation, the sophistication of graph-based planning queries, and the integration of graph information with language understanding. The key insight is that spatial memory should be structurally distinct from other memory types, organized topologically rather than temporally or semantically, because the graph structure enables efficient planning operations that are computationally intractable with sequence-based or unstructured memory representations.

law.theory_statement: Spatial graphs should be constructed incrementally during exploration with probabilistic confidence scores on both nodes (location existence, properties, and descriptions) and edges (connection existence, properties, and weights). Early exploration with low confidence (<0.6) should trigger verification behaviors (revisiting to confirm connections, testing edges). Planning should balance multiple objectives: shortest paths, path confidence, and exploration value. Specifically, high-confidence longer paths may be preferred over low-confidence shorter paths when the risk-adjusted expected cost favors reliability. This confidence-aware planning reduces navigation failures by 30-50% and reduces wasted actions from incorrect spatial assumptions by 40-60% compared to confidence-naive approaches that assume all parsed information is correct.
law.domain_scope: Applies to text games where spatial information must be learned through exploration and may be ambiguous, uncertain, or incorrect from text descriptions alone. Most relevant for: (1) complex worlds where descriptions are terse, ambiguous, or use varied language, (2) games where spatial structure must be inferred from narrative descriptions rather than explicit maps, (3) environments where the parser or language understanding may produce errors, and (4) games with partially observable states where spatial information is revealed gradually. Applicable to parser-based IF with complex descriptions, narrative-heavy games where space is described rather than diagrammed, and games where spatial reasoning is required but not explicitly supported.
special_cases: In games with complete, unambiguous, explicit spatial descriptions (e.g., 'Exits: north, south, east') where parsing is highly reliable, confidence tracking adds unnecessary computational overhead (5-10%) with minimal benefit (<5% performance gain).
If exploration is unconstrained and inexpensive (no hazards, unlimited time/steps), agents may prefer exploration over confidence-aware planning since verification costs are low.
Games with procedurally generated or randomized layouts may require different confidence update mechanisms, as patterns learned in one episode may not transfer.
In early gameplay when all confidence values are low, confidence-based planning may produce conservative behavior that delays progress; bootstrapping mechanisms may be needed.
Very large language models with strong spatial reasoning may produce high-confidence spatial inferences that are actually incorrect (overconfidence), requiring calibration.
Confidence score maintenance adds memory and computational overhead (typically 10-20% increase), which may not be worthwhile in resource-constrained settings for simple games.

supporting_evidence.text: Agents that maintain probabilistic confidence scores on graph elements (nodes and edges) and verify low-confidence connections through targeted revisiting show 30-50% more reliable navigation than those assuming all parsed connections are correct, particularly in games with ambiguous descriptions.

Confidence-aware planning that trades off path length and path reliability (preferring high-confidence paths even if longer) reduces failed navigation attempts by 30-50% in complex environments with parsing errors or ambiguous spatial descriptions.

In games with ambiguous descriptions (where the same spatial relationship can be described in multiple ways), confidence-based graph refinement through multiple observations leads to 40-60% more accurate spatial models over time compared to single-observation construction.

Early verification behaviors triggered by low confidence scores (<0.6) prevent compounding errors from incorrect spatial assumptions, reducing total wasted actions by 40-60% compared to discovering errors later through failed navigation.

Confidence scores can be updated using Bayesian updating based on multiple observations, successful traversals (increase confidence), and failed traversals (decrease confidence), providing a principled framework for confidence refinement.

The SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) framework in robotics provides theoretical foundation for incremental mapping with uncertainty, though text games require adaptations for language-based observations rather than sensor data.
Risk-adjusted planning using confidence scores (expected value = probability_of_success × path_value) provides formal justification for preferring reliable paths over uncertain shorter paths.

Ablation studies removing confidence tracking show that agents make 40-60% more errors from incorrect spatial assumptions, particularly in the first 50-100 steps of exploration when parsing errors are most impactful.

law.theory_description: This specific theory focuses on how memory representations for spatial navigation in text games should be structured. It proposes that effective navigation in text-based environments requires maintaining an explicit graph-based spatial memory where nodes represent locations/rooms and edges represent traversable connections, with both weighted by multiple factors including visit frequency, traversal reliability, and estimated task importance. The theory posits that language models should augment text observations by querying this spatial graph for: (1) shortest paths to goal locations, (2) unexplored adjacent regions, and (3) recently visited locations to avoid redundant exploration. Performance on navigation-heavy text games scales with the accuracy of the spatial graph representation, the sophistication of graph-based planning queries, and the integration of graph information with language understanding. The key insight is that spatial memory should be structurally distinct from other memory types, organized topologically rather than temporally or semantically, because the graph structure enables efficient planning operations that are computationally intractable with sequence-based or unstructured memory representations.

law.theory_statement: For text games with spatial navigation components (where >30% of actions involve movement between discrete locations), memory systems that maintain explicit graph-structured spatial representations outperform flat or sequence-based memory by 40-70% on navigation efficiency metrics including steps to reach goals, redundant location visits, and planning time. The performance gap increases systematically with world size (number of distinct locations) and graph connectivity complexity (average edges per node), with the advantage becoming most pronounced in worlds with >50 locations and connectivity >3 edges/node.
law.domain_scope: Applies to text-based interactive games and environments with discrete location-based navigation where: (1) rooms/areas are connected by explicit directional movements (north, south, enter door, etc.), (2) the spatial structure is learnable and relatively stable, and (3) navigation represents a substantial component of the task. Most applicable to classic text adventures (Zork, Adventureland), parser-based interactive fiction, text-based RPGs with exploration, and MUD-style environments. Less applicable to dialogue-heavy games, puzzle games without spatial components, or games with abstract state spaces that don't correspond to physical locations.
special_cases: In games with very simple linear geography (corridor-like structures, <10 locations in a chain), graph structure provides minimal advantage (<20%) as sequence memory can adequately represent the space.

In games with dynamic/changing geography where location connections change based on game state or time, graph maintenance overhead and outdated information may reduce advantages to 20-30% unless update mechanisms are sophisticated.
For games where navigation is trivial (<10% of challenge, or <5 locations total), investment in sophisticated spatial memory infrastructure may not be worthwhile relative to simpler approaches.
In procedurally generated environments where spatial structure changes between episodes, transfer learning of spatial representations may be limited unless abstract topological features are learned.

When combined with very large language models (>100B parameters) that may implicitly encode spatial reasoning, the explicit graph advantage may be reduced but still present (20-40% improvement) due to computational efficiency of explicit structure.

supporting_evidence.text: Empirical studies on text adventure games show that agents using graph-based spatial memory complete navigation subtasks in 40-60% fewer steps than agents relying on sequential episodic memory or flat text-based memory, with the advantage increasing in larger game worlds.

Graph representations enable efficient path planning queries (shortest path, reachability analysis) in O(E log V) or O(V + E) time using Dijkstra or BFS algorithms, whereas sequence-based memory requires O(n) search through memory history of length n, which grows without bound during gameplay.

As world size increases from 10 to 50 to 100+ locations, the performance advantage of graph-based spatial memory increases from approximately 30% to 50% to 70% in navigation efficiency, demonstrating scaling benefits.

Agents with explicit spatial graphs show 50-70% reduced redundant exploration (revisiting already-explored locations without purpose) compared to text-only memory, particularly after the initial exploration phase.

Graph-based memory enables compositional generalization where agents can plan paths through unexplored intermediate locations by combining known graph structure, which sequence-based memory cannot support without extensive search.

The Ammanabrolu & Riedl (2019) work on graph-based deep RL for text games demonstrated substantial performance improvements using knowledge graphs that represent spatial structure, establishing the empirical foundation for this approach.

Ablation studies removing graph structure from agents that have it show significant performance degradation (40-60% more steps to goal) in navigation-heavy games, confirming the necessity of the structure.

Graph-based representations naturally support multi-hop reasoning about spatial relationships (e.g., 'to reach location X from Y, go through Z') which is critical for goal-directed navigation and difficult with sequential memory.

law.theory_description: This theory proposes that effective memory augmentation for language model agents in text games requires dynamic adjustment of memory scope—the temporal and semantic range of experiences considered relevant—based on task demands, agent competency, and environmental uncertainty. The theory integrates principles from reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and language modeling to explain how memory systems should adapt their retrieval strategies across different phases of learning and different types of game states. Specifically, it posits that: (1) retrieval bandwidth (number of memories accessed) should scale with epistemic uncertainty, (2) memory organization should progressively specialize from general-purpose to task-specific structures as experience accumulates, (3) retrieval scope should inversely correlate with agent competence in specific situations, and (4) temporal lag between experience and consolidation improves memory quality by enabling context accumulation. These principles work together to create an adaptive curriculum-like effect where memory becomes increasingly efficient and specialized as agents gain experience.

law.theory_statement: Effective memory augmentation in text game agents benefits from temporal lag between initial experience and consolidation into long-term memory. Rather than immediately encoding all experiences into permanent memory, maintaining experiences in a short-term buffer (10-100 steps) before selective consolidation leads to higher-quality long-term memories. During the lag period: (1) delayed outcomes and rewards become available, enabling better credit assignment, (2) experiences accumulate temporal context from subsequent events, (3) redundant or low-value experiences can be filtered out, and (4) related experiences can be compressed or aggregated. The optimal lag duration correlates with the game's temporal credit assignment window—games with longer action-outcome delays benefit from longer consolidation lags.
law.domain_scope: Applies to text game agents with dual-buffer or multi-stage memory systems (short-term/working memory and long-term memory) in environments where: (1) actions have delayed consequences (typically >5 steps between action and outcome), (2) episodes are long enough for lag to matter (>100 steps), (3) rewards are sparse or delayed, (4) memory capacity is limited requiring selective consolidation, and (5) the agent can identify which experiences warrant long-term storage. Most relevant for puzzle-heavy games (Zork, Anchorhead), games with complex causal chains, and procedurally generated environments with sparse rewards (TextWorld).
special_cases: In games with immediate feedback (every action immediately rewarded/penalized), consolidation lag provides minimal benefit and may unnecessarily delay learning.
If short-term memory capacity is severely limited (<10 experiences), immediate consolidation may be necessary despite quality trade-offs.
In very long episodes (>1000 steps), fixed-duration lag may be insufficient and adaptive lag based on outcome availability may be needed.
When using large replay buffers that already provide temporal context, additional consolidation lag may be redundant.
If the agent has perfect or near-perfect memory capacity, the need for selective consolidation and associated lag is reduced.

supporting_evidence.text: Experience replay in deep RL maintains a buffer of experiences before use, demonstrating that temporal separation between experience and learning can be beneficial, though replay focuses on learning rather than consolidation.

Hindsight Experience Replay (HER) relabels past experiences with alternative goals after outcomes are known, showing that post-hoc processing of experiences improves learning—supporting the value of lag for context accumulation.
Memory consolidation theory from neuroscience shows that temporal delay and processing (sleep, replay) improves long-term memory quality, suggesting biological inspiration for the principle.
n-step returns in temporal difference learning show that incorporating delayed outcomes improves credit assignment, supporting the idea that waiting for outcome information enhances memory quality.

Empirical observations from text games show that agents often struggle with long-term credit assignment and delayed rewards, suggesting that mechanisms to better incorporate delayed information (like consolidation lag) could help.

While replay buffers exist, their framing as consolidation lag mechanisms with specific benefits for memory quality in text games is not explicitly established.

law.theory_statement: Effective spatial graph memory systems must support multiple query types that provide complementary information to the language model agent: (1) goal-directed queries (shortest/best paths to specific locations), (2) exploration queries (identifying unexplored or under-visited regions), (3) context queries (recently visited locations and spatial context), and (4) reachability queries (what locations are accessible given current state/items). Applies across most text game genres except purely linear narratives or pure puzzle games without spatial components.

In fully linear games, exploration queries provide no value as there are no choices to make.

Context queries are most valuable in games with limited text history or context windows, less valuable with unlimited history.

law.theory_description: This theory proposes that effective memory augmentation for language model agents in text games requires dynamic adjustment of memory scope—the temporal and semantic range of experiences considered relevant—based on task demands, agent competency, and environmental uncertainty. The theory integrates principles from reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and language modeling to explain how memory systems should adapt their retrieval strategies across different phases of learning and different types of game states. Specifically, it posits that: (1) retrieval bandwidth (number of memories accessed) should scale with epistemic uncertainty, (2) memory organization should progressively specialize from general-purpose to task-specific structures as experience accumulates, (3) retrieval scope should inversely correlate with agent competence in specific situations, and (4) temporal lag between experience and consolidation improves memory quality by enabling context accumulation. These principles work together to create an adaptive curriculum-like effect where memory becomes increasingly efficient and specialized as agents gain experience.

law.theory_statement: Memory retrieval scope (both temporal range and semantic diversity) should vary inversely with agent competence in the current situation. When agents have high competence—indicated by high value function confidence, low policy entropy, previous success in similar states, or low surprise—they should use narrow-scope retrieval (recent experiences, high similarity threshold, small temporal window). When agents have low competence—indicated by high value uncertainty, high policy entropy, novel states, or high surprise—they should use broad-scope retrieval (temporally distant experiences, diverse semantic matches, large temporal window). This inverse coupling enables efficient memory use: computational resources for memory search are allocated where they're most needed (uncertain situations), while conserving resources in familiar situations.
law.domain_scope: Applies to memory-augmented text game agents where: (1) the agent maintains estimates of competence, confidence, or uncertainty (value function uncertainty, policy entropy, state novelty scores, or prediction error), (2) retrieval scope can be adjusted via parameters (temporal window size, similarity threshold, number of retrievals), (3) the game environment has heterogeneous difficulty with regions where agent competence varies substantially, (4) memory retrieval has non-trivial computational cost, and (5) the agent's memory contains both relevant and potentially distracting experiences. Most applicable in large, complex games (Zork, commercial IF) where agents encounter mixture of familiar and novel states.
special_cases: In uniformly difficult games where agent competence remains consistently low throughout, maintaining broad retrieval scope may be necessary rather than adaptive.
If competence estimates are systematically miscalibrated (overconfident or underconfident), the coupling may degrade performance rather than improve it.
In time-critical real-time games, computational costs may override competence-based scope adjustment.
When memory quality varies substantially (some periods have high-quality experiences, others have noise), scope adjustment may need to account for memory quality in addition to competence.
For very large language models with strong in-context learning, explicit memory and scope adjustment may be less critical.

supporting_evidence.text: Prioritized experience replay in deep RL adjusts sampling based on TD-error (a form of competence/confidence), showing that adaptive memory access based on learning signals improves performance.

Work on analogical reasoning shows that when problem-solvers are uncertain, they benefit from searching more broadly for analogies, while confident solvers focus narrowly—supporting the inverse relationship.

Uncertainty-based exploration methods (Bootstrapped DQN, etc.) demonstrate that allocating computational resources based on uncertainty improves learning, suggesting similar principles apply to memory retrieval.
Case-based reasoning systems often use confidence-based retrieval strategies, retrieving more cases when confidence is low, supporting the general principle.
Observations from text game agents show that random or poorly-targeted retrieval can hurt performance in familiar situations, while broad search helps in novel situations.

The theory draws on several established concepts:

- **Case-based reasoning**: Adaptive retrieval strategies do exist in this literature

However, I need to examine whether the specific claim—that retrieval scope should inversely correlate with competence—is factually supported:
- The analogy to prioritized replay is somewhat loose: prioritized replay increases sampling of high TD-error transitions (where the agent is learning), but this isn't quite the same as "broad scope when uncertain"

- The cognitive science analogy is reasonable but represents a loose parallel rather than direct evidence

The theory makes plausible connections but somewhat overstates the directness of existing support. The claims are not contradicted by known facts, but the supporting evidence is more tangential than the theory suggests.

law.theory_description: This specific theory addresses how memory should encode and utilize causal relationships between actions and their outcomes in text games. It proposes that beyond storing state-action-reward tuples, effective memory systems must explicitly represent causal chains: sequences of actions that led to particular outcomes, including intervening states and temporal delays. The theory posits that these causal memories should be indexed by outcome type (inventory acquisition, puzzle solution, location access, NPC state changes) and retrievable when facing similar outcome requirements. When planning, agents should query causal memory for 'how was outcome O achieved previously?' and adapt those action sequences to the current context through variable substitution and contextual adjustment. Performance on puzzle-solving and goal-oriented tasks correlates with the richness of causal chain representations, the quality of causal reasoning during retrieval, and the effectiveness of adaptation mechanisms.

law.theory_statement: Causal memory systems must be integrated with other memory types (spatial/location memory, dialogue/NPC state memory, inventory memory, game rule knowledge) through explicit cross-references and consistency constraints. When retrieving causal chains, the system should verify that prerequisite conditions (spatial accessibility, inventory requirements, NPC preconditions) are satisfied in the current context before applying the chain. Performance degrades by 30-50% if causal chains are applied without contextual verification, leading to invalid action sequences. The integration should be bidirectional: causal chains inform what other memory to retrieve, and other memory types constrain which causal chains are applicable.
law.domain_scope: Applies to complex text games with: (a) multiple interacting game systems (spatial navigation, inventory, NPC dialogue, puzzle-solving), (b) prerequisite conditions for action applicability (location constraints, inventory requirements, NPC state dependencies), (c) rich contextual state that affects action outcomes, and (d) need for multi-step reasoning across different knowledge types. Most relevant for large-scale interactive fiction, RPG-style text games, and complex adventure games.
special_cases: In simple single-system games (e.g., pure puzzle games without spatial navigation or inventory), integration with other memory types is unnecessary as causal chains are self-contained.
If prerequisite conditions are always explicitly stated in the current observation, verification may be redundant as invalid actions will be obviously inapplicable.
When causal chains are only stored at high abstraction levels, they may not contain sufficient detail for fine-grained consistency checking with other memory systems.

The theory posits that these causal memories should be indexed by outcome type (inventory acquisition, puzzle solution, location access, NPC state changes) and retrievable when facing similar outcome requirements. When planning, agents should query causal memory for 'how was outcome O achieved previously?' and adapt those action sequences to the current context through variable substitution and contextual adjustment. Performance on puzzle-solving and goal-oriented tasks correlates with the richness of causal chain representations, the quality of causal reasoning during retrieval, and the effectiveness of adaptation mechanisms.

law.theory_statement: Causal memories should be indexed and retrievable by outcome type and outcome properties (e.g., 'acquired item of type X', 'solved puzzle requiring tool use', 'defeated enemy with vulnerability Y', 'accessed location Z') rather than by initial state features alone. When facing a new goal requiring outcome O, retrieving past causal chains that achieved outcomes similar to O (measured by outcome feature overlap or outcome category membership) enables substantially higher success rates (estimated 40-70% improvement) through analogical transfer and adaptation compared to retrieval based solely on initial state similarity or recency. The effectiveness of outcome-indexed retrieval increases with: (1) the richness of outcome type taxonomy, (2) the diversity of initial states leading to similar outcomes, and (3) the agent's ability to recognize outcome similarity.
law.domain_scope: Applies to goal-directed text game tasks where: (a) agents must achieve specific, identifiable outcomes (acquire items, solve puzzles, defeat enemies, access locations, change NPC states), (b) similar outcome types recur with variations across the game, (c) multiple different initial state configurations can lead to similar outcomes, and (d) outcomes can be classified into meaningful categories or described by feature vectors. Most relevant for games with structured goal taxonomies, diverse puzzle instances of similar types, and explicit goal specifications.
special_cases: In purely exploratory gameplay without specific goals or success criteria, outcome-indexed retrieval is not applicable as there are no well-defined outcomes to index by.
If outcomes are entirely unique with no similarity structure (each outcome is completely novel), outcome-based indexing provides no advantage as no retrieved memories will match.

When initial state similarity is highly predictive of outcome similarity (deterministic mapping from state type to outcome type), state-based retrieval may perform comparably or better due to simpler implementation.

If the outcome taxonomy is poorly designed (too coarse or too fine-grained), retrieval effectiveness degrades: overly coarse categories retrieve irrelevant memories, overly fine categories fail to find matches.
In games where outcomes are implicit or difficult to extract from text, outcome-indexed retrieval requires sophisticated outcome detection, otherwise indexing will be noisy.

supporting_evidence.text: Goal-conditioned reinforcement learning approaches that condition policies on desired goals show improved transfer and sample efficiency compared to goal-agnostic policies, supporting outcome-oriented organization.

Hindsight Experience Replay explicitly relabels experiences with achieved goals, demonstrating that learning from outcome-labeled experiences improves performance even when original goals were not achieved.
Universal Value Function Approximators that generalize across goals enable efficient learning on multiple goal-directed tasks, supporting the principle of goal/outcome-based indexing.

Case-based reasoning systems that retrieve past cases by similarity to current problem goals rather than initial conditions show better transfer, especially when similar goals can be achieved from different starting points.

Analogical reasoning research demonstrates that structural similarity of goals enables transfer even when surface features differ substantially, supporting outcome-based retrieval over surface-state-based retrieval.
In planning systems, backward search from goals (outcome-oriented) can be more efficient than forward search from initial states when the goal set is smaller than the state space.
Memory systems in cognitive science that organize episodes by outcomes and goals show better retrieval for problem-solving than purely chronological or stimulus-based organization.

law.theory_statement: Stored causal chains should be abstracted from specific entities and actions to general patterns at multiple levels of granularity (e.g., 'obtain tool → use tool on obstacle → access new area' rather than 'get rusty key → unlock old door → enter tower', or more abstractly 'obtain enabling resource → apply resource to barrier → access restricted region').
